question: What is software engineering and how does it fit into computer science?
answer_guidelines: To answer this question, you may find it useful to re - read Section 1.1.  Software engineering is the study or practice of using computers and computing technology to solve real - world problems.  Computer scientists study the structure, interactions and theory of computers and their functions.  Software engineering is a part of computer science in that software engineers use the results of studies to build tools and techniques to meet the needs of customers.
ground_truth: Software engineering focuses on using computers and computing technology as problem-solving tools to address real-world problems [1]. While computer scientists investigate the structure, theory, and interactions of computers and programming languages, software engineers apply these theoretical results to build tools, techniques, and systems that meet the needs of customers [1, 2]. Therefore, software engineering fits into computer science in a way similar to how chemical engineering fits into chemistry: it takes the theoretical foundations of the discipline and applies them practically to solve general problems [2].
source_text: As software engineers, we use our knowledge of computers and computing to help solve problems. Often the problem with which we are dealing is related to a computer or an existing computer system, but sometimes the difficulties underlying the problem have nothing to do with computers. [...] To understand how a software engineer fits into the computer science world, let us look to another discipline for an example. Consider the study of chemistry and its use to solve problems. The chemist investigates chemicals: their structure, their interactions, and the theory behind their behavior. Chemical engineers apply the results of the chemist’s studies to a variety of problems. Chemistry as viewed by chemists is the object of study. On the other hand, for a chemical engineer, chemistry is a tool to be used to address a general problem (which may not even be “chemical” in nature). We can view computing in a similar light. We can concentrate on the computers and programming languages, or we can view them as tools to be used in designing and implementing a solution to a problem. Software engineering takes the latter view, as shown in Figure 1.3. Instead of investigating hardware design or proving theorems about how algorithms work, a software engineer focuses on the computer as a problem-solving tool.

question: What is the difference between technical and business quality? Explain why each is important.
answer_guidelines: Technical qual ity emphasizes the technical performance of a software product.  Often, it is measured by the number of faults, failures and timing problems.  Business quality focuses on the value of the software product for the business.  It is measured by return on inve stment (ROI).  ROI may be viewed very differently depending on the organization.  In the Brodman and Johnson (1995) study, different views of ROI were found with the U.S. government and U.S. industry.  The U.S. government views ROI in terms of dollars save d while U.S. industry views ROI in terms of effort savings. Both technical and business quality are important.  A software product may have technical quality in that it performs the way it is intended or specified to perform.  But, if the software system is not used for business functions, the system is not providing value to the business.  In this case, the system would have technical quality, but not business quality.  Similarly, a software product can provide functionality that is vital to the business,  yet the technical quality may be poor.  Ideally, a software product should have both technical and business quality. You may find it useful to re - read Section 1.3.
ground_truth: Technical quality evaluates the internal and operational performance of a software product, often measured by mathematical expressions involving the number of faults, failures, and timing issues [3]. Business quality, on the other hand, evaluates the software based on its overall value to the organization providing products and services [3], typically measured by return on investment (ROI) [4]. The perspective on ROI can vary; for example, the government views ROI in terms of dollar savings, whereas industry tends to view it in terms of effort savings [5, 6]. Both forms of quality are important because they serve complementary goals. A product with high technical quality performs according to its specifications, but if it does not solve the fundamental business problem or provide a clear return (such as cost/effort savings or increased processing capacity), it lacks business quality and provides no real value to the organization [7]. Conversely, a system might offer vital business functionality but fail due to poor technical quality. High-quality software must balance both aspects to be truly successful.
source_text: When the focus of quality assessment is on products and processes, we usually measure quality with mathematical expressions involving faults, failures, and timing. Rarely is the scope broadened to include a business perspective, where quality is viewed in terms of the products and services being provided by the business in which the software is embedded. That is, we look at the technical value of our products, rather than more broadly at their business value, and we make decisions based only on the resulting products’ technical quality. [...] We measure the quality [of our software] by the obvious metrics: up versus down time, maintenance costs, costs connected with modifications, and the like. [...] If there is not clear (expected) business value (expressed quantitatively: number of claims processed, etc.) then we can’t launch a systems project. [...] return on investment (ROI), a concept that is clearly defined in the business community. [...] The government views ROI in terms of dollars, looking at reducing operating costs, predicting dollar savings, and calculating the cost of employing new technologies. On the other hand, industry viewed investment in terms of effort, rather than cost or dollars.

question: Give two or three examples of fail ures you have encountered while using software.  Describe how these failures affected the quality of the software product.
answer_guidelines: Answers to this question will vary depending upon your experiences.  In your answer, you should include the following: *  A description of the failure.  Explain how the system performed in a way that was different from its required behavior. *  A list of quality characteristics that have been violated by the failure. You may find it useful to us e McCall's quality model (from Figure 1.5 of the textbook) as a checklist.  That is, use the items listed in the model to ask questions about the failures you describe. For example, was the failure that you experienced related to correctness?  Were the res ults incomplete or inconsistent?  Did the failure affect the system's usability?  These are a sample of the questions that you may want to consider.
ground_truth:
Example 1: While using a mobile banking application, I transferred funds between accounts, but the dashboard failed to update the new balances until I manually logged out and logged back in. This failure departed from the system's required real-time synchronization behavior. Based on McCall's quality model, this violated 'Correctness' (inconsistent results) and 'Reliability' (accuracy). 

Example 2: A video editing software I used crashed completely and shut down without saving when I attempted to import an unsupported video file format. This failure departed from the expectation that the system would handle exceptions gracefully. This violated 'Reliability' (lack of error tolerance) and 'Usability' (poor operability and communicativeness), as the system should have safely handled the bad input with an error message instead of a hard crash.
source_text:
A failure is a departure from the system’s required behavior. It can be discovered before or after system delivery, during testing, or during operation and maintenance. [1] 

Figure 1.5 McCall’s quality model.
Correctness (Completeness, Traceability, Consistency)
Reliability (Accuracy, Error tolerance, Consistency, Simplicity)
Efficiency (Execution efficiency, Storage efficiency)
Integrity (Access control, Access audit)
Usability (Operability, Training, Communicativeness)
Maintainability
Testability
Flexibility
Portability
Reusability
Interoperability [2, 3]

question: Examine failures that have occurred in software that you have written.  Identify and list the faults and errors that caused each failure.
answer_guidelines: Answers will be specific to the types of failures that you identify.  The purpose of this exercise is to m ake the distinction between errors, faults and failures clear.  Review the definitions for errors, faults and failures.  These definitions can be found in Sidebar 1.1 of the textbook.
ground_truth:
Example from a personal programming project where I wrote a Python script to calculate the average test scores of students in a class:

- Failure: The system crashed with a 'ZeroDivisionError' and halted execution when evaluating a new student who had not yet taken any exams. This was a departure from the required behavior of gracefully skipping students without grades.
- Fault: The code contained a division operation (`total_score / num_exams`) without a condition checking if the denominator (`num_exams`) was zero. This logic omission embedded in the code is the fault.
- Error: The human mistake (error) was my conceptual oversight during the design phase; I incorrectly assumed every student would have at least one exam score and failed to account for the edge case of a new student.
source_text:
A fault occurs when a human makes a mistake, called an error, in performing some software activity. For example, a designer may misunderstand a requirement and create a design that does not match the actual intent of the requirements analyst and the user. This design fault is an encoding of the error, and it can lead to other faults, such as incorrect code and an incorrect description in a user manual. Thus, a single error can generate many faults, and a fault can reside in any development or maintenance product. 

A failure is a departure from the system’s required behavior. [1, 4]

question: Look through several issues of software magazines (IEEE Computer and IEEE Software are good choices) from the 1970's, 1980's and recent issues.  Compare the types of problems and solutions described in the older issues with those described in the more  recent issues.
answer_guidelines: Answers to this question will vary depending upon which articles are in volved.  To answer this question, you may want to use the seven key factors that have altered software engineering (from Wasserman (1996) and presented in Section 1.8 of the textbook) to make your comparison among articles from the past and recent articles .  In your comparison, cite specific examples of how the problems and solutions have changed.
ground_truth:
When comparing older software magazines from the 1970s and 1980s to recent issues, the shift in problems and solutions aligns heavily with Wasserman's seven key factors that have altered software engineering. Older articles typically addressed problems related to centralized mainframe computing, where input was linear (e.g., punch cards) and output was alphanumeric. Solutions often revolved around strict adherence to the linear waterfall model and optimizing code to save expensive hardware resources.

In contrast, recent issues address entirely different problem spaces. Using Wasserman's factors, the shift is evident:
1. Criticality of time-to-market: Recent articles focus heavily on rapid delivery and agile methods due to intense commercial pressures, rather than prolonged, rigid schedules.
2. Shifts in economics: Solutions now prioritize developer time (e.g., using high-level languages and frameworks) over hardware constraints, as hardware costs have plummeted while development effort has become the major expense.
3 & 4. Desktop computing and extensive networking: Problems have shifted to distributed systems, client-server architectures, and web/network security, driven by the ubiquity of local/wide-area networking and personal devices.
5. Object-oriented technology: Discussions shifted from procedural decomposition to OO design patterns, inheritance, and reusable components.
6. Graphical user interfaces: Solutions now extensively cover GUIs (windows, icons, menus) instead of text-based batch processing interfaces.
7. Unpredictability of the waterfall model: Recent articles largely discard the rigid waterfall model in favor of iterative, spiral, and agile process models to accommodate constantly changing user requirements.
source_text:
Wasserman (1995) points out that the changes since the 1970s have been dramatic. For example, early applications were intended to run on a single processor, usually a mainframe. The input was linear, usually a deck of cards or an input tape, and the output was alphanumeric. The system was designed in one of two basic ways: as a transformation, where input was converted to output, or as a transaction, where input determined which function would be performed. Today’s software-based systems are far different and more complex... 

In his Stevens lecture, Wasserman (1996) summarized these changes by identifying seven key factors that have altered software engineering practice... 
1. criticality of time-to-market for commercial products 
2. shifts in the economics of computing: lower hardware costs and greater development and maintenance costs 
3. availability of powerful desktop computing 
4. extensive local- and wide-area networking 
5. availability and adoption of object-oriented technology 
6. graphical user interfaces using windows, icons, menus, and pointers 
7. unpredictability of the waterfall model of software development [5, 6]

question: Describe the process you use to get to ready for class or work in the morning. Draw a diagram to capture the process.
answer_guidelines: When answering this question, consider the definition of a process. Your answer should include the following: * the activities involved * the steps required to complete the tasks * the inputs and outputs to each activity * the constraints involved You may find it useful to re - read Section 2.1.
ground_truth:
The process of getting ready for work involves a sequence of activities subject to specific constraints to produce an intended outcome. 
- Activities and Steps: The process begins with waking up when the alarm triggers. The next steps involve showering (washing), getting dressed (selecting clothes and putting them on), and eating breakfast (preparing and consuming food).
- Inputs: A sleepy, unwashed person, clean clothes, and raw breakfast ingredients.
- Outputs: An awake, clean, dressed person, and a consumed breakfast.
- Constraints: The entire process must be completed by 8:00 AM to ensure timely arrival at work, and the shower is constrained by a limited supply of hot water.

(Diagram Description: [Alarm Triggers/Input: Sleepy Person] -> (Activity: Wake Up) -> (Activity: Shower) -> (Activity: Get Dressed) -> (Activity: Eat Breakfast) -> [Output: Ready for Work]. All enclosed by a timeline boundary constraint of finishing before 8:00 AM).
source_text: We can think of a set of ordered tasks as a process: a series of steps involving activities, constraints, and resources that produce an intended output of some kind. ... The process uses resources, subject to a set of constraints (such as a schedule), and produces intermediate and final products. ... Each process activity has entry and exit criteria, so that we know when the activity begins and ends. The activities are organized in a sequence, so that it is clear when one activity is performed relative to the other activities.

question: Describe three software development life - cycle models. For each, name the main activities performed, and the inputs and outputs of each activity. For each give an example of the kind of software development project where the life - cycle model would be well - suited, and an example of where the life - cycle model would be inappropriate; explain why.
answer_guidelines: Answers to this question will vary depending upon the life - cycle models chosen. Section 2.2 describes several life - cycle process models. Your answer should include the activities, the inputs and the outputs involved with each process model. In addition, you should provide examples and reasons why a particular process model would be appropriate as well as situations where a process model would be inappropriate. For example, if a development project is highly risky (development team is inexperienced with the domain, time pressures exist) a spiral life - cycle model would be appropriate because development activities are combined with risk management to minimize and control risk. However, if the development project is low risk, a spiral model may not be the best choice.
ground_truth:
1. Waterfall Model: 
- Activities: Requirements analysis, system design, program design, coding, testing, delivery, maintenance.
- Inputs/Outputs: Customer needs (input) -> Requirements specification (output); Specification (input) -> Design documents (output); Design (input) -> Code (output).
- Appropriate: Well-understood, low-risk projects with stable requirements (e.g., building a standard payroll system where the rules are fixed).
- Inappropriate: Projects with high uncertainty or changing requirements, because it lacks the flexibility to revisit earlier phases easily without significant disruption.

2. Prototyping Model:
- Activities: Prototype requirements, prototype design, prototype system test, revise prototype.
- Inputs/Outputs: Informal/incomplete requirements (input) -> Prototype (output); User feedback (input) -> Revised requirements/design (output).
- Appropriate: Projects where customers are unsure of what they want or need, such as a novel user interface for a new consumer application.
- Inappropriate: Highly predictable, strictly regulated systems (like standard accounting software) where all requirements are known upfront and strict adherence to a pre-defined standard is more important than exploration.

3. Spiral Model:
- Activities: Determine goals/alternatives/constraints, evaluate alternatives and risks, develop and test (prototype), plan next phase.
- Inputs/Outputs: Initial plan/budget (input) -> Risk analysis/Prototype (output); Prototype (input) -> Validated requirements/design (output).
- Appropriate: High-risk, complex projects where continuous risk assessment and mitigation are necessary (e.g., a massive new aerospace defense system).
- Inappropriate: Small, low-risk, or low-budget projects where the overhead of rigorous risk analysis in every cycle outweighs the project's overall benefits.
source_text:
One of the first models to be proposed is the waterfall model, illustrated in Figure 2.1, where the stages are depicted as cascading from one to another ... one development stage should be completed before the next begins. ... The biggest problem with the waterfall model is that it does not reflect the way code is really developed. Except for very well-understood problems, software is usually developed with a great deal of iteration.
Since the prototyping model allows all or part of a system to be constructed quickly to understand or clarify issues, it has the same objective as an engineering prototype... The overall goal remains the same: reducing risk and uncertainty in development.
Boehm (1988) viewed the software development process in light of the risks involved, suggesting that a spiral model could combine development activities with risk management to minimize and control risk. ... The spiral model is an example of such a dynamically changing process, responding to feedback from early activities in order to reduce risk in later ones.

question: What is the difference between static and dynamic modeling? Explain how each type of modeling is useful.
answer_guidelines: A static process model describes the elements of a process. It depicts where the inputs are transformed to outputs. A dynamic process model enacts the process and allows the user to view how the products are transformed over time. A static model is useful to identify the elements of the process. A dynamic model may be useful to simulate how changes to the process affect the outputs of the process over time. For more details on static and dynamic process models, re - read Section 2.3.
ground_truth:
A static model depicts the structural elements of a process, establishing what happens by showing how inputs are transformed into outputs. It is useful for defining the basic elements of the process, understanding relationships among roles, activities, and artifacts, and forming a common understanding of the development workflow (e.g., using Lai notation to describe artifact states and transition rules).

In contrast, a dynamic model enacts the process over time, simulating how products are transformed and how resources flow through activities. This is highly useful for predictive planning and optimization. It allows project managers to 'test out' process choices—such as observing the effects of adding more testers or changing a schedule—before actual resources and money are expended.
source_text: A static model depicts the process, showing that the inputs are transformed to outputs. A dynamic model enacts the process, so the user can see how intermediate and final products are transformed over time. ... A desirable property of a process model is the ability to enact the process, so that we can watch what happens to resources and artifacts as activities occur. In other words, we want to describe a model of the process and then watch as software shows us how resources flow through activities to become outputs. This dynamic process view enables us to simulate the process and make changes before the resources are actually expended. For example, we can use a dynamic process model to help us decide how many testers we need or when we must initiate testing in order to finish on schedule.

question: Use the five desirable properties of process modeling tools and techniques identified by Curtis, Kellner and Over(1992) and presented in Section 2.4 of the textbook to evaluate one process modeling tool or technique. You may use an example from the book and/or consult outside sources.
answer_guidelines: Your answer to this question will depend upon which process modeling technique or tool is chosen. Your answer should address the five desirable properties of process modeling tools and techniques outlined in Section 2.4. Does the tool or technique you are evaluating possess the desirable characteristic? Which features of the tool or technique satisfy the desirable property? Are there areas where the tool or technique lacks support for a desirable property?
ground_truth:
Evaluating the Marvel Specification Language (MSL) against the five desirable properties of process modeling tools:

1. Facilitates human understanding and communication: MSL represents processes using object-oriented definitions, rules, and tool envelopes. However, it was found that separating the information model from the behavioral model was necessary to keep the resulting model clear and concise for human understanding.
2. Supports process improvement: MSL strongly supports this. In documented case studies, simulating MSL models allowed developers to identify missing task inputs, ambiguous criteria, and inefficiencies, enabling significant process improvement without a major investment in resources.
3. Supports process management: MSL allows the process to be highly project-specific (e.g., modeling specific AT&T networks and workcenters), which helps project managers reason about the process structure and track trouble ticket artifacts across various states.
4. Provides automated guidance in performing the process: By generating "process enactment environments" populated with instances of the information model's classes, MSL guides the workflow according to strict rule-based specifications of process behavior.
5. Supports automated process execution: MSL uses "tool envelopes" to interface seamlessly between the Marvel process environment and external software tools used to execute the process, capturing relevant data and enforcing rules during execution.
source_text:
Curtis, Kellner, and Over (1992) have identified five categories of desirable properties: 1. Facilitates human understanding and communication. ... 2. Supports process improvement. ... 3. Supports process management. ... 4. Provides automated guidance in performing the process. ... 5. Supports automated process execution.
In both studies, the researchers used MSL, the Marvel Specification Language, to define the process, and then generated a Marvel process enactment environment for it... MSL uses three main constructs—classes, rules, and tool envelopes—to produce a three-part process description: 1. a rule-based specification of process behavior 2. an object-oriented definition of the model’s information process 3. a set of envelopes to interface between Marvel and external software tools used to execute the process. ... The modeling and simulation exercises were useful for early problem identification and resolution. ... Barghouti and his colleagues note the importance of dividing the process modeling problem into two pieces: modeling the information and modeling the behavior. By separating these concerns, the resulting model is clear and concise.

question: Explain the difference between prescriptive and descriptive process models. What is the purpose for each? When is it appropriate to use each?
answer_guidelines: Descriptive models attempt to describe what is actually happening in the process. Prescriptive process models attempt to describe what should be happening with the process. For more details on prescriptive and descriptive process models, you may find it helpful to re - read Section 2.2. In your answer to this question, use the reasons for modeling a process (in Section 2.2) to describe how and when prescriptive and descriptive models are useful. Can you think of cases where a prescriptive process model may be inappropriate? How does a descriptive model help in building a prescriptive model?
ground_truth:
Prescriptive process models outline how software development *should* progress, serving as guidelines or frameworks to enforce consistency, structure, and specific development goals. Descriptive process models, on the other hand, depict how software development is *actually* done in reality, capturing the practical, day-to-day activities, iterations, and workarounds of a team.

Descriptive models are appropriate when a team needs to evaluate and understand its current workflow. By describing the process as it actually happens, the team forms a common understanding of their activities and can easily spot inconsistencies, redundancies, and omissions. Prescriptive models are appropriate when an organization wants to standardize its development cycle, establish formal controls, or tailor a proven methodology to meet specific project goals (like finishing on schedule or finding faults early).

Highly prescriptive process models may be inappropriate for projects characterized by high uncertainty, rapidly changing business needs, or novel problems. For instance, agile practitioners argue that concentrating on responding to change is more effective than strictly following a prescribed plan, as it is often impossible to anticipate all requirements at the beginning of development. Additionally, great variations among application types and organizational cultures can make it impossible to be strictly prescriptive about the process itself.

A descriptive model helps in building a prescriptive model by making the gap between "what should be" and "what is" visible. By documenting the actual process (descriptive), a team can note and correct real-world inefficiencies and omissions, ultimately tailoring a targeted prescriptive model that is realistic, more effective, and focused on building the final product.
source_text:
Many process models are described in the software engineering literature. Some are prescriptions for the way software development should progress, and others are descriptions of the way software development is done in actuality. In theory, the two kinds of models should be similar or the same, but in practice, they are not. Building a process model and discussing its subprocesses help the team understand the gap between what should be and what is.

There are several other reasons for modeling a process:
* When a group writes down a description of its development process, it forms a common understanding of the activities, resources, and constraints involved in software development.
* Creating a process model helps the development team find inconsistencies, redundancies, and omissions in the process and in its constituent parts. As these problems are noted and corrected, the process becomes more effective and focused on building the final product.
* The model should reflect the goals of development, such as building high-quality software, finding faults early in development, and meeting required budget and schedule constraints. As the model is built, the development team evaluates candidate activities for their appropriateness in addressing these goals...
* Every process should be tailored for the special situation in which it will be used. Building a process model helps the development team understand where that tailoring is to occur.

[...]
the great variations among application types and organizational cultures make it impossible to be prescriptive about the process itself.

[...]
They concentrate on responding to change rather than on creating a plan and then following it, because they believe that it is impossible to anticipate all requirements at the beginning of development.

question: Describe the process of getting a degree (bachelor's, master's or PhD) as a work breakdow n structure. Draw an activity graph for the process. What is the critical path?
answer_guidelines: The answer to this question will depend on the degree chosen and the process involved. Your work breakdown structure should include phases, steps, activities and milestones. You must also consider constraints such as time limits and pre - requisites. For example, to get a PhD, you may have to complete coursework, pass comprehensive exams, pass a prelim inary exam, and defend your dissertation. There may be constraints, such as a two year time limit to complete all coursework or all comprehensive exams must be successfully completed before a preliminary exam can occur. For more details on work breakdown structures, activity graphs and critical paths, re - read Section 3.1.
ground_truth:
To define the process, we must distinguish between activities and milestones: "An activity is a part of the project that takes place over a period of time, whereas a milestone is the completion of an activity—a particular point in time" [1]. Creating a work breakdown structure helps "depict the project as a set of discrete pieces of work" [2].

**Work Breakdown Structure for a Master's Degree:**
*   **Phase 1: Coursework**
    *   Step 1.1: Core Classes (Activity: Take 4 core classes. Milestone 1.1: Core requirements met).
    *   Step 1.2: Electives (Activity: Take 4 elective classes. Milestone 1.2: Electives met).
*   **Phase 2: Master's Thesis**
    *   Step 2.1: Proposal (Activity: Write and defend proposal. Milestone 2.1: Proposal accepted).
    *   Step 2.2: Research & Writing (Activity: Conduct research and draft thesis. Milestone 2.2: Thesis draft complete).
    *   Step 2.3: Defense (Activity: Defend thesis. Milestone 2.3: Degree awarded).

**Activity Graph & Constraints:**
An activity graph depicts the dependencies among these milestones [3]. A major constraint is that Phase 2 (Thesis) cannot be fully completed until Phase 1 (Coursework) is finished, although Step 2.1 (Proposal) can happen concurrently with Step 1.2 (Electives). 

**Critical Path:**
"The longest path has a slack of zero for each of its nodes, because it is the path that determines whether or not the project is on schedule. For this reason, it is called the critical path" [4]. The critical path reveals the "minimum amount of time it will take to complete the project" [5]. In this scenario, the critical path is the sequential track that takes the longest: Completing the Coursework (which takes the maximum duration of 4 semesters), followed by the Research & Writing (1 semester), and Defense (part of the final semester). Because coursework dictates the timeline and has zero slack, it forms the critical path alongside the final thesis defense.
source_text:
An activity is a part of the project that takes place over a period of time, whereas a milestone is the completion of an activity—a particular point in time. [1]
Analysis of this kind is sometimes described as generating a work breakdown structure for a given project, because it depicts the project as a set of discrete pieces of work. [2]
We can draw an activity graph to depict the dependencies; the nodes of the graph are the project milestones, and the lines linking the nodes represent the activities involved. [3]
The paths can show us the minimum amount of time it will take to complete the project, given our estimates of each activity’s duration. Moreover, CPM reveals those activities that are most critical to completing the project on time. [5]
The longest path has a slack of zero for each of its nodes, because it is the path that determines whether or not the project is on schedule. For this reason, it is called the critical path. [4]

question: Describe the organizational structure for your work environment. Classify the working styles of several of your co - workers. What are the advantages to this structure? Do y ou see any problems with the current structure?
answer_guidelines: Your answer will depend on your work environment. Use the descriptions of organizational structures to characterize your work environment. In Section 3.2, chief programmer teams are d escribed. Section 3.6 describes several management structures such as matrix organizations and integrated product development teams. When evaluating the structure, you should consider whether the environment is highly or loosely structured. You should c onsider the number of potential lines of communication. When describing the working styles of your co - workers, keep in mind the styles described in the chapter: rational introverts, rational extroverts, intuitive introverts, and intuitive extroverts. Con sider how these working styles affect communication in your work environment.
ground_truth:
In my hypothetical work environment, our team is organized using an "egoless" approach within a loosely structured Integrated Product Development Team. In an egoless approach, the team "holds everyone equally responsible" and decisions are "democratic, and all team members vote on a decision" [6].

**Working Styles of Co-workers:**
*   **Co-worker A** is a *rational extrovert*. They tend to "assert their ideas and not let 'gut feeling' affect their decision making" [7]. They focus on the bottom line, rely on logic rather than emotion, and tell others their thoughts quickly and efficiently [7].
*   **Co-worker B** is an *intuitive introvert*. They are "sensitive and aware of her emotional reactions to people and problems" [8]. They take time to make decisions to ensure they are the right ones, acting as a good listener and analyzing relational dependencies [8].

**Advantages of this Structure:**
A democratic, egoless structure is highly advantageous when "there is much uncertainty involved in a project" and requirements might change [9]. The encouragement of open communication, participation in decision-making, and a loosely defined hierarchy fosters creativity and allows the team to adapt to new techniques and shifting client needs effectively [9, 10].

**Problems with this Structure:**
A major problem with a loosely structured, democratic team is the exponential growth of communication paths. "If a project has n workers, then there are n(n - 1)/2 pairs of people who might need to communicate" [11]. Reaching a consensus takes significant time, and for projects with a "high degree of certainty, stability, uniformity, and repetition," a hierarchical structure (like a chief programmer team) would be far more efficient [12].
source_text:
The rational extroverts tend to assert their ideas and not let “gut feeling” affect their decision making. They tell their colleagues what they want them to know... When reasoning, they rely on logic, not emotion. [7]
Ying, an intuitive introvert, also thrives on her colleagues’ attention. She is sensitive and aware of her emotional reactions to people and problems... [8]
Instead of a single point of responsibility, an egoless approach holds everyone equally responsible. ... The egoless team structure is democratic, and all team members vote on a decision [6]
On the other hand, when there is much uncertainty involved in a project, a more democratic approach may be better. ... participation in decision making, a loosely defined hierarchy, and the encouragement of open communication can be effective. [9]
if a project has n workers, then there are n(n - 1)/2 pairs of people who might need to communicate [11].
projects with a high degree of certainty, stability, uniformity, and repetition can be accomplished more effectively by a hierarchical organizational structure such as the chief programmer team. [12]

question: Discuss two techniques for making a prediction for effort. In particular, explain where during the development process the prediction is made, and when (if at all) the prediction is repeated.
answer_guidelines: The chapter covers several different techniques for predicting effort. Algorithmic models and machine - learning models are presented in Section 3.3. Project planning is covere d in Section 3.8.
ground_truth:
Two prominent techniques for predicting effort are Algorithmic Models (such as COCOMO II) and Machine-Learning Methods (such as Neural Networks or Case-Based Reasoning).

**1. Algorithmic Models (e.g., COCOMO II):**
Algorithmic models express the relationship between effort and the factors that influence it through mathematical equations (e.g., Effort = (a + bS^c)m(X), where S is system size) [13]. COCOMO II predicts effort dynamically across three major stages of development: 
*   *Stage 1 (Application Composition):* Made very early when building prototypes to resolve high-risk issues. Size is estimated using "application points" [14].
*   *Stage 2 (Early Design):* Made when alternative architectures are explored. Size is estimated using "function points" [15].
*   *Stage 3 (Postarchitecture):* Made once development has actually begun. Size is estimated in function points or lines of code, adjusting for numerous cost drivers [16]. 
The prediction is inherently repeated and refined as the project progresses through these three stages.

**2. Machine-Learning Methods:**
These methods utilize historical data to train a system to predict future effort. For instance, Case-Based Reasoning (CBR) involves identifying a new problem as a "case," retrieving similar cases from a historical repository, and reusing that past knowledge to suggest an effort solution [17, 18]. Similarly, Neural Networks are "trained" with past project data to recognize patterns between inputs (like team size or complexity) and outputs (effort) [19].

**Timing and Repetition:**
For both techniques, initial cost and effort estimation must be done "as early as possible during the project's life cycle, since it affects resource allocation and project feasibility" [20]. Because effort estimation is characterized by high uncertainty initially, estimates "should be done repeatedly throughout the life cycle; as aspects of the project change, the estimate can be refined, based on more complete information about the project’s characteristics" [20].
source_text:
Cost, schedule, and effort estimation must be done as early as possible during the project’s life cycle, since it affects resource allocation and project feasibility. ... But estimation should be done repeatedly throughout the life cycle; as aspects of the project change, the estimate can be refined, based on more complete information about the project’s characteristics. [20]
Researchers have created models that express the relationship between effort and the factors that influence it. The models are usually described using equations, where effort is the dependent variable, and several factors (such as experience, size, and application type) are the independent variables. [13]
The COCOMO II estimation process reflects three major stages of any development project. ... At stage 1, projects usually build prototypes ... COCOMO II estimates size in what its creators call application points. [14] At stage 2, the early design stage, a decision has been made to move forward ... COCOMO II employs function points as a size measure. [15] By stage 3, the postarchitecture stage, development has begun, and far more information is known. In this stage, sizing can be done in terms of function points or lines of code [16]
some researchers are looking to machine learning for assistance in producing good estimates. For example, neural networks can represent a number of interconnected, interdependent units, so they are a promising tool for representing the various activities involved in producing a software product. [21] Neural networks are developed by “training” them with data from past projects. [19]
A machine-learning technique called Case-Based Reasoning (CBR) can be applied to analogy-based estimates. Used by the artificial intelligence community, CBR builds a decision algorithm based on the several combinations of inputs that might be encountered on a project. ... Estimation using CBR involves four steps: 1. The user identifies a new problem as a case. 2. The system retrieves similar cases from a repository of historical information. 3. The system reuses knowledge from previous cases. 4. The system suggests a solution for the new case. [17, 18]

question: Any prediction generates an estimate, E, that can be compared eventually to an actual value, A. Name two values that can be calculated from E and A to help determine the accuracy of the estimating process. Define the two values and discuss how the values for each are used to tell us that a prediction is acceptable.
answer_guidelines: Two measures of an estimate's accuracy are the mean magnitude of relative error (MMRE) and the percent of projects with estimate values within x percent of the actual value (PRED). The MMRE is the average of |E-A|/A for each project. T he PRED(x) is n/N where n is the number of projects with |E-A|/A < x and N is the total number of projects. When the MMRE < 0.25, the technique is considered fairly good. Some researchers would like the MMRE to be less than 10%. For the PRED, a PRED(0.2 5) > 0.75 is considered good. The PRED(0.25) criterion means that 75% of the project estimates were within 25% of the actual values.
ground_truth: Two values that can be calculated from the estimated value (E) and actual value (A) to help determine the accuracy of the estimating process are PRED and MMRE [1]. PRED(x/100) measures the percentage of projects for which the estimate is within x% of the actual value [1]. For example, PRED(0.25) tells us what percentage of project estimates were within 25% of the actual values, and a model is considered to function well if PRED(0.25) is greater than 75% [1]. MMRE stands for the mean magnitude of relative error [1]. A prediction technique is often considered fairly good if the MMRE is 0.25 or less, though some researchers suggest that an acceptable MMRE should be 0.10 or less [1].
source_text: There are two statistics that are often used to help you in assessing the accuracy, PRED and MMRE. PRED(x/100) is the percentage of projects for which the estimate is within x% of the actual value. For most effort, cost, and schedule models, managers evaluate PRED(0.25), that is, those models whose estimates are within 25% of the actual value; a model is considered to function well if PRED(0.25) is greater than 75%. MMRE is the mean magnitude of relative error, so we hope that the MMRE for a particular model is very small. Some researchers consider an MMRE of 0.25 to be fairly good, and Boehm (1981) suggests that MMRE should be 0.10 or less.

question: Describe two different size measures and the advantages and disadvantages of using each.
answer_guidelines: When estimating effort, it is often necessary to estimate size. Examples of size measures include lines of code, function points and object points. Object points can be calculated early in the process, but object points are a coarse measurement. Lines of code are not available early in the process, but are relatively easy to calculate. Function points can be calculated ea rlier than lines of code and provide a richer system description than object points.
ground_truth: Two different size measures used in effort estimation are **lines of code** and **function points** [2-4]. The advantage of **lines of code** is that it provides a concrete measurement of the final product's size, but the disadvantage is that it is impossible to know this metric accurately early in the development cycle [2, 4]. By contrast, **function points** can be calculated earlier in the process, such as during the early design stage, because they estimate the functionality captured in the requirements [3]. An advantage of function points is that they offer a richer system description than more coarse, early-stage measurements like application points (or object points) [3]. A disadvantage of function points is that they cannot be applied in the very earliest prototyping stages when the system's functionality is not yet fully specified, which is when application points must be used instead [2, 3].
source_text: The COCOMO II estimation process reflects three major stages of any development project. Whereas the original COCOMO model used delivered source lines of code as its key input, the new model acknowledges that lines of code are impossible to know early in the development cycle. At stage 1, projects usually build prototypes... COCOMO II estimates size in what its creators call application points. This size measure is an extension of the object-point approach... At stage 2, the early design stage... COCOMO II employs function points as a size measure. Function points... estimate the functionality captured in the requirements, so they offer a richer system description than application points. By stage 3, the postarchitecture stage, development has begun, and far more information is known. In this stage, sizing can be done in terms of function points or lines of code...

question: Most of a system's requirements specify that the system should do what it is intended to do. Is it also appropri ate to specify that the system should not do what it is not intended to do? If your answer is no, explain why; if your answer is yes, give an example.
answer_guidelines: An example of the latter type of requirement is a security requirement. In this case, it is necessary to specify exactly what the system should NOT allow a user or other system to do.
ground_truth: Yes, it is highly appropriate and often necessary to specify what a system should not do. A primary example of this is a security requirement, which specifies strict boundaries on access and behavior, such as ensuring that access to the system or information is controlled, and that users or external programs are isolated from other users' data or the operating system to prevent theft or vandalism [1]. Another example is a safety property, which is an invariant property specifying that a particular bad behavior never happens; for instance, a requirement in a turnstile system might dictate that the number of entries into the zoo is never more than the number of entry fees paid (or conversely, no one should enter without paying) [2, 3].
source_text:
Security
* Must access to the system or information be controlled?
* Should each user’s data be isolated from the data of other users?
* Should user programs be isolated from other programs and from the operating system?
* Should precautions be taken against theft or vandalism?

Our turnstile example has two requirements: (1) no one should enter the zoo without paying an entrance fee, and (2) for every entrance fee paid, the system should not prevent a corresponding entry. 

A safety property is an invariant property that specifies that a particular bad behavior never happens; for example, a safety property of the turnstile problem is that the number of entries into the zoo is never more than the number of entry fees paid.

question: Describe the different consumers of software requirements i.e. the different users, or types of users, o f a software requirements document). For each consumer, explain how he or she would use the requirements, and how the requirements should be documented to make them useful for this consumer.
answer_guidelines: Requirements need to be used by: a. The customer, who should check that the system desc ribed actually matches his or her needs. For use by the customer, requirements should be easy to understand, with a minimum of jargon, to facilitate clear communication with the customer. b. Designers, who need to construct a design of the system described in the requirements. The requirements will need to be as complete, clear, and correct as possible so that designs developed from it are correct. Also, they will need to identify all of the constraints on the system so that the design can correctly incorporat e them. c. Testers, who need to develop test scripts. To support testers the requirements should be as precise as possible, so that the values that need to be tested and the expected system behavior are well -specified. d. Documentation writers, who will write th e user manuals based on the requirements. As for the customer, the requirements should clearly communicate the features of the system.
ground_truth:
Software requirements are consumed by several different groups, each using the documentation for distinct purposes: 
1. Customers, clients, and users use the requirements to ensure that the described system actually matches their needs and expectations [4]. For these consumers, the requirements definition should be written in the customer's vocabulary, referring to objects, states, events, and activities in the real world rather than technical jargon [5, 6].
2. Designers treat requirements as constraints to construct an acceptable architectural and module-level design [4]. For designers, the requirements specification must be as complete, clear, and correct as possible, specifying behaviors exactly in terms of the system's interfaces [5, 6].
3. Testers derive a suite of acceptance tests from the requirements to demonstrate that the delivered system functions as requested [4]. To support testers, the requirements must be precise and testable so they can adequately evaluate the implementation [6].
4. Documentation writers use the specifications to write user manuals and training materials, requiring that the features and functions of the system be clearly communicated [6].
5. The maintenance team uses the requirements to ensure that any future system enhancements or repairs do not interfere with the system's original intent [4].
source_text:
In the end, the requirements are used by many different people and for different purposes. Requirements analysts and their clients use requirements to explain their understanding of how the system should behave. Designers treat requirements as constraints on what would be considered an acceptable solution. The test team derives from the requirements a suite of acceptance tests, which will be used to demonstrate to the customer that the system being delivered is indeed what was ordered. The maintenance team uses the requirements to help ensure that system enhancements (repairs and new features) do not interfere with the system’s original intent.

Where the requirements definition is written in terms of the customer’s vocabulary, referring to objects, states, events, and activities in the customer’s world, the requirements specification is written in terms of the system’s interface.

At the same time, the requirements part of your team must work closely with
* Customers and users, so that your team builds a product that serves their needs
* Designers, so that they construct a design that fulfills the requirements specification
* Testers, so that their test scripts adequately evaluate whether the implementation meets the requirements
* Documentation writers, so that they can write user manuals from the specifications

question: One source of problems in the requirements phase can be the rela tionship between system developers and their customers. What are some negative stereotypes customers may hold about developers? What could you, as a developer on a project, do to minimize the impact of those negative stereotypes?
answer_guidelines: Use Table 4.5 as a starting point for your answer. For each point listed on the table under the category of “How users s ee developers,” think about whether or not these prejudices are in fact true, and if so, if there is a reason they must be that way; if not, ask what you could do to change that perception. For example, the first point, “Developers don't understand operati onal needs,” is true in many cases; software engineers don't always have extensive training in the customer's application domain or way of doing business. But, a serious effort by the developer to learn about the customer's needs in order to support the cu stomer is not only helpful for reversing negative stereotypes, it is a prerequisite for building quality software.
ground_truth:
According to Scharer's study on how users and developers view each other, customers and users often hold several negative stereotypes about developers. They may believe that developers: do not understand operational needs, cannot translate clearly stated needs into a successful system, set unrealistic standards for requirements definition, place too much emphasis on technicalities, are always late and over budget, cannot respond quickly to legitimately changing needs, say "no" all the time, try to dictate how users should do their jobs, and ask users for too much time and effort to the detriment of their primary duties [7]. 

To minimize the impact of these stereotypes, developers must make a serious effort to learn about the customer's application domain, business needs, and jargon [8, 9]. Since software engineers don't always have extensive training in the customer's domain, working collaboratively to elicit requirements (e.g., observing the current system, apprenticing with users, or building prototypes) helps reverse the perception that developers don't understand operational needs [10-12]. Establishing this mutual understanding without getting bogged down in technicalities is a prerequisite for building quality software and breaking down these prejudicial boundaries [7, 13].
source_text:
TABLE 4.1 How Users and Developers View Each Other (Scharer 1990)
How Users See Developers
Developers don’t understand operational needs. 
Developers can’t translate clearly stated needs into a successful system. 
Developers set unrealistic standards for requirements definition. 
Developers place too much emphasis on technicalities. 
Developers are always late. 
Developers can’t respond quickly to legitimately changing needs. 
Developers are always over budget. 
Developers say “no” all the time. 
Developers try to tell us how to do our jobs. 
Developers ask users for time and effort, even to the detriment of the users’ important primary duties.

Customers are not always good at describing exactly what they want or need, and we are not always good at understanding someone else’s business concerns.The customers know their business, but they cannot always describe their business problems to outsiders; their descriptions are full of jargon and assumptions with which we may not be familiar. Likewise, we as developers know about computer solutions, but not always about how possible solutions will affect our customers’ business activities.

question: Download and read the Rob ertsons' requirements definition template (from http://www.atlsysguild.com). Write a short report in which you summarize for a software developer how he or she can use the template to validate requirements. Your report should address practical concerns abo ut how to use the template in the requirements process. For example, you should address questions such as: At what point in the requirements process can the template be helpful? What activities are necessary in order to use the template for validating requ irements? What skills will a developer have to possess in order to apply the template effectively? What types of errors and faults will the template help uncover?
answer_guidelines: The template is useful during requirements reviews, but it can also be helpful if given to the writers of requirements as a guide for what i nformation should be included. The categories and items of the template can be used as a checklist for ensuring that all of the appropriate issues have been addressed and the correct information has been included in the requirements document. The reviewer of the requirements will have to have a sufficient understanding of the requirements in order to find the pertinent information for each item of the template. The template will be most helpful for finding defects of omission, that is, for identifying types of information that should be included in the requirements but were left out.
ground_truth: The Robertsons' requirements definition template, associated with the Volere process model, serves as a comprehensive tool for eliciting and checking a set of requirements [1]. For a software developer, this template is most helpful during the requirements review phase, where representatives from the development staff and the customer examine the requirements document to identify problems [2]. However, it can also be introduced earlier as a structural guide for the writers of the requirements to ensure that all necessary information is captured from the start [1]. To use the template for validation, developers can treat its categories and items as a rigorous checklist; checking against such lists is a proven technique to guide the search for errors [3]. To apply the template effectively, the reviewer must possess a strong understanding of both the problem domain and the system's operational needs to correctly locate and evaluate the pertinent information for each template item [2]. By cross-referencing the requirements document against the template's exhaustive categories, the template is exceptionally useful for finding defects of omission—specifically, identifying critical types of information (like environmental assumptions, specific constraints, or quality requirements) that should have been documented but were inadvertently left out [3].
source_text:
A comprehensive requirements-definition template developed by James and Suzanne Robertson can be found at the Web site of the Atlantic Systems Guild: http:// www.systemsguild.com. This template is accompanied by a description of the Volere process model, which is a complete process for eliciting and checking a set of requirements. [1]

Depending on the definition techniques that we use, some of the above checks (e.g., that the requirements are consistent or are traceable) may be automated. Also, common errors can be recorded in a checklist, which reviewers can use to guide their search for errors. [3]

More often, the requirements are validated in a requirements review. In a review, representatives from our staff and the customer’s staff examine the requirements document individually and then meet to discuss identified problems. [2]

question: Pamela Zave has proposed a classification scheme for organizing the different types of rese arch that go on in the area of software requirements (P. Zave (1997). “Classification of research efforts in requirements engineering.” ACM Computing Surveys, 29(4): 315 - 321). Choose one of the categories she presents and write a brief (one paragraph) des cription of how research in this area is of use to software developers. Track down one of the papers she cites as an example of research in this category, and summarize the problem it addresses and the results it presents.
answer_guidelines: Answers will vary depending on the category chosen and papers selected.
ground_truth: One prominent category of requirements research related to Pamela Zave's work involves "operational specifications" and the explicit modeling of environmental assumptions. Research in this area is highly useful to software developers because it addresses the difficult transition between informal real-world needs and formal system behaviors. By defining executable requirements or explicitly documenting assumptions about how the real world behaves, developers can evaluate the implications of the requirements early in the development process and resolve uncertainties before design begins [4, 5]. An excellent example of research in this domain is Zave and Jackson's (1997) paper, "Four dark corners of requirements engineering" [6]. This paper addresses the widespread problem of undocumented assumptions about the environment in which the software operates [5]. It distinguishes between the desired behavior to be realized by the proposed system and the existing behavior of the environment that remains unchanged (often called domain knowledge) [5]. The research demonstrates that unless these environmental assumptions are formally maintained as part of the requirements documentation, developers risk building a system that fails when exposed to real-world conditions [5]. The result of this work is a clearer methodological boundary between the system and its environment, ensuring that specifications truly satisfy customer requirements [5].
source_text:
Zave (1984) suggests a process model that allows the developers and customers to examine the requirements and their implications early in the development process, where they can discuss and resolve some of the uncertainty. In the operational specification model, the system requirements are evaluated or executed in a way that demonstrates the behavior of the system. [4]

Zave and Jackson (1997) have looked carefully at problems in software requirements and specification, including undocumented assumptions about how the real world behaves. There are actually two types of environmental behavior of interest: desired behavior to be realized by the proposed system (i.e., the requirements) and existing behavior that is unchanged by the proposed system. The latter type of behavior is often called assumptions or domain knowledge. [5]

Zave, Pamela, and Michael Jackson (1997). “Four dark corners of requirements engineering.” ACM Transactions on Software Engineering and Methodology, 6(1) (January): 1–30. [6]

question: The following statements describe modules in a (hypothetical) program. F or each, decide whether the module is likely to have a high or low degree of cohesion. If cohesion is low, explain why. a. Module “InventorySearchByID” searches the records in inventory to see if any match the specified range of ID numbers. A data structure is returned containing any matching records. b. Module “ProcessPurchase” removes the purchased product from inventory, prints a receipt for the customer and updates the log. c. Module “FindSet” processes the user's request, determine s the set of items from inventory that match the request, and formats the items into a list that can be shown to the customer.
answer_guidelines: a. Module “InventorySearchByID” can be expected to have high cohesion; it performs only one type of functionality (a search). b. Module “ProcessPurchase” can be expected to have relatively low cohesion, since it involves very different functionalities: prin ting a receipt for use by the user is logically quite different from updating a data store. c. Module “FindSet”can be expected to have relatively low cohesion, since it invokes very different functionalities: parsing input, a search through data, and output formatting.
ground_truth:
Cohesion measures the dependence within and among a module's internal elements. A highly cohesive module serves a singular purpose and contains only the elements essential to that function. Based on this:

a. Module "InventorySearchByID" has a high degree of cohesion (specifically functional cohesion) because it performs only one clearly defined task: searching the inventory for a specific ID range.

b. Module "ProcessPurchase" has a low degree of cohesion. It combines several disjointed tasks—updating a data store (inventory), interacting with a user interface/hardware (printing a receipt), and modifying a system record (updating the log). These distinct operations mean the module serves multiple purposes, reducing its cohesion.

c. Module "FindSet" also has a low degree of cohesion. It groups together the disparate activities of parsing user input, executing a data search, and formatting the output. Because it encompasses both data manipulation and data display routines, it risks needing to evolve in multiple, conflicting directions.
source_text: In contrast to measuring the interdependence among multiple modules, cohesion refers to the dependence within and among a module’s internal elements (e.g., data, functions, internal modules). The more cohesive a module, the more closely related its pieces are, both to each other and to the module’s singular purpose. A module that serves multiple purposes is at greater risk of its elements needing to evolve in different ways or at different rates. For example, a module that encompasses both data and routines for displaying those data may change frequently and may grow in different directions, as new uses of the data require both new functions to manipulate data and new ways of visualizing them. Instead, our design goal is to make each module as cohesive as possible, so that each module is easier to understand and is less likely to change. [...] Our ideal is functional cohesion, where two conditions hold: all elements essential to a single function are contained in one module, and all of that module’s elements are essential to the performance of that function. A functionally cohesive module performs only the function for which it is designed, and nothing else.

question: What does it mean to say that a design review should be “egoless”? Why are egoless reviews necessary? Suggest some s teps that may help achieve egoless reviews.
answer_guidelines: “Egoless” reviews occur when criticisms are directed at the design process and the design itself, not at the designers and other participants. Egoless reviews are useful since they remind participants that they are moving toward a common goal a nd keep them focused on the software under review, rather than encouraging them to make excuses or defend themselves. Egoless reviews enhance communication and allow more time to be spent discussing the software itself. Egoless reviews can be facilitated b y any measure that reminds participants that the software and not the individuals are under discussion. Examples include making a statement of the review goal at the beginning of the review, or making sure that comments during the review remain centered on the software. Egoless reviews are also facilitated by not inviting a representative of management, to make sure that participants do not feel their performance is being judged based on other participants' comments.
ground_truth:
An "egoless" design review means that all criticisms, questions, and discussions are directed exclusively at the product (the design) and the process, rather than at the individuals who created it. 

Egoless reviews are necessary because they prevent developers from feeling defensive or personally attacked, ensuring that the focus remains on the shared goal of finding faults and improving the software's integrity. When egos are removed from the equation, communication is enhanced among the diverse members of the team, and time is spent productively analyzing the software rather than assigning blame.

Steps to achieve egoless reviews include:
1. Clearly stating the goals of the review at the beginning of the meeting (e.g., reminding everyone that the goal is fault detection, not performance evaluation).
2. Framing questions and comments around the software artifacts rather than the person (e.g., "This module's coupling seems high" instead of "Why did you design it this way?").
3. Excluding management or supervisory personnel from the review to ensure participants do not feel their job performance is being judged based on the flaws discovered.
source_text: In general, the point of the design review is to detect faults rather than correct them. It is important to remember that those who participate in the review are investigating the integrity of the design, not of the designers. Thus, the review is valuable in emphasizing to all concerned that we are working toward the same goal. The criticism and discussion during the design review are egoless, because comments are directed at the process and the product, not at the participants. The review process encourages and enhances communication among the diverse members of the team. [...] Remember that you are criticizing the architecture, not the architect, and that software development works best when egos are left out of the discussion.

question: Choose a software system that you use and for which there is some feature of the interface that you, as a user, dislike. Briefly describe the software and why you consider this feature of the interface to be a pr oblem. Speculate as to whether the problem could have been avoided during design. If so, what changes to the design process would have been necessary?
answer_guidelines: Answers will vary depending on software system chosen and interface problem discussed. In any case, you should first make sure that the problem you've selected is in fact a problem from the user's point of view (i.e. one that results from a poorly defined interface). For example, suppose you wo rk with a piece of software that, at certain times, will not allow you to interact with the system while some computation is going on. The fact that you have to wait for the computation to complete may not be a user interface problem; the computation may s imply require a large amount of time to complete the calculation. However, if the user is not given a chance to cancel the computation after it has started, then a frustrating problem can occur in which a user has decided that it is not necessary to run th e computation at this point but has to wait for it to complete anyway. Many such problems can be caught in the design phase if resources are expended on user interface design and review.
ground_truth: (Note: The specific software system is drawn from outside personal experience, as prompted by the question, but the analysis uses concepts from the sources). An example of this is a web-based airline booking system where, after clicking "Search Flights", the interface locks up with a loading screen and provides no "Cancel" button. If the user immediately realizes they typed the wrong date, they are forced to wait for the lengthy database computation to complete before they can fix their mistake [1]. This is a user interface design problem because the architecture fails to support user-initiated generic commands like "cancel" or "undo" [2]. This problem could have been easily avoided during the design phase if more resources were expended on user interface design and review [1]. To prevent this, the designers should have developed scenarios for each way the user interacts with the system [3] and utilized user interface prototyping to evaluate the "look and feel" with actual users before implementation [4]. Including an active design review where reviewers specifically look for handling of user exceptions would have also exposed this missing architectural support [5].
source_text:
Answers will vary depending on software system chosen and interface problem discussed. In any case, you should first make sure that the problem you've selected is in fact a problem from the user's point of view (i.e. one that results from a poorly defined interface). For example, suppose you work with a piece of software that, at certain times, will not allow you to interact with the system while some computation is going on. The fact that you have to wait for the computation to complete may not be a user interface problem; the computation may simply require a large amount of time to complete the calculation. However, if the user is not given a chance to cancel the computation after it has started, then a frustrating problem can occur in which a user has decided that it is not necessary to run the computation at this point but has to wait for it to complete anyway. Many such problems can be caught in the design phase if resources are expended on user interface design and review. [1]

Second, there are some user-initiated commands that require architectural support. These include generic commands such as cancel, undo, aggregate, and show multiple views... At a minimum, the system needs a process that listens for these commands, because they could be generated at any time, unlike user commands that are input in response to a system prompt. [2]

Let us look more closely at designing a user interface. We must consider several issues: identifying the humans who will interact with the system, defining scenarios for each way that the system can perform a task... [3]

It is appropriate for the designers to work with users to decide how to design the system's interface. This may require developing several prototypes to show users the possibilities, to determine how performance requirements can be met, or to evaluate the best “look and feel.” [4]

question: Find a paper or book that deals with design patterns in more detail. Summarize briefly one of the patte rns presented; explain what it is useful for, when it can be used, and its effects on the rest of the program. What are some of the difficulties you might expect to encounter if using design patterns in practice?
answer_guidelines: The definition of a design pattern almost always includes context in formation, a definition of the pattern itself, and tradeoffs associated with its use, so be sure to address each of these points in your summary. Design patterns suffer from some of the same difficulties that are experienced in any reuse situation: it is often hard to recognize situations that could benefit from reuse, and it is difficult to search a repository of components (including patterns) to find the best match to the current situation. As with any other type of reuse, developers may not be sufficie ntly motivated to overcome these difficulties with design patterns.
ground_truth:
Gamma et al. (1995) present a foundational catalogue of design patterns [6]. One such pattern is the Observer pattern, which is highly useful when software needs to notify multiple objects of key events without hard-coding the dependencies between the publisher and the subscribers [7]. For example, in a text editor where a document is displayed in a main window, a thumbnail, and a toolbar, the Observer pattern synchronizes these views by allowing them to register as observers; when the document changes, it publishes an update notification to all registered modules [7]. The effect on the rest of the program is improved modularity and decoupling of the client modules from the core object. 

However, using design patterns in practice presents several difficulties. First, because patterns often introduce additional classes, associations, and method calls (indirection), they can make the design seem overly complicated and degrade runtime performance [8]. Second, design patterns suffer from the same obstacles as any reuse situation: it can be very difficult for a developer to recognize a situation that would benefit from a specific pattern, and searching a repository to find the best match for the current problem is challenging [9]. Finally, because of this complexity and search effort, developers may lack sufficient motivation to overcome these difficulties and might choose to write ad-hoc code instead [9].
source_text:
The definition of a design pattern almost always includes context in formation, a definition of the pattern itself, and tradeoffs associated with its use, so be sure to address each of these points in your summary. Design patterns suffer from some of the same difficulties that are experienced in any reuse situation: it is often hard to recognize situations that could benefit from reuse, and it is difficult to search a repository of components (including patterns) to find the best match to the current situation. As with any other type of reuse, developers may not be sufficiently motivated to overcome these difficulties with design patterns. [9]

The Observer pattern is an application of the publish–subscribe architecture style. It is particularly useful when our software needs to notify multiple objects of key events, and we do not want to hard-code into our solution which objects are to be notified... We could employ a more efficient design by having the system publish notifications of updates, and having modules register to receive the notifications. [7]

Design patterns make substantial use of interfaces, information hiding, and polymorphism, and they often introduce a level of indirection in a clever way. Because the patterns add extra classes, associations, and method calls, they sometimes can seem overly complicated... For this reason, the patterns are useful only in situations where the extra flexibility is worth the extra cost required to implement them. [8]

question: Read the account by Nancy Leveson and Clark Turner of the Therac - 25 accidents (N. Leveson and C. Turner (1993). “An investigation of the Therac - 25 accidents.” IEEE Computer, 26(7) (July): 18 - 41). Give a brief summary of some of the important lessons that can be learned for design.
answer_guidelines: The Leveson and Turner paper contains many lessons that can be applied to design. You should select some of these lessons, and cite experiences with the Therac - 25 that support them. For ex ample, page 39 contains a list of five basic software engineering principles that were violated in development of the Therac - 25; which of these can be applied to design? What kind of conditions did their absence from the development of the Therac - 25 lead t o? You should use these principles as a starting point but also identify other lessons. For example, what kind of lessons can be found about reuse? About timing problems?
ground_truth:
The investigation of the Therac-25 accidents by Leveson and Turner highlights several critical lessons for software design.
