First, the software design violated fundamental engineering principles: the design was unnecessarily complex. A key lesson is that simple designs are easier to understand, code, and test than complex ones [10]. Second, the software lacked defensive mechanisms; software should be designed to anticipate failures and proactively capture information about them [10]. 

Furthermore, the Therac-25 accidents serve as a stark warning about the dangers of blind software reuse. The machine reused software from an older model (the Therac-6), but the Therac-25 relied entirely on software for safety, having removed the hardware interlocks that the older machine possessed [11]. This proves that reusing a component without fully analyzing its new context—especially in safety-critical systems—can lead to catastrophic conditions [11], [12]. 

Finally, the root cause of the massive radiation overdoses was an underlying timing error that was extremely difficult to reproduce [10]. This underscores the critical lesson that designs handling concurrency and user input must carefully account for timing issues, and that relying solely on system-level testing is insufficient; extensive component-level testing and formal analysis are required before integration [10].
source_text:
Leveson and Turner (1993) describe the machine, the accidents, and the software issues in great detail... The software was written by a single person, using PDP-11 assembly language and reusing code from an earlier machine called the Therac-6. Some of the software was tested on a simulator, but most of it was tested as part of the larger system... [11]

Leveson and Turner (1993) describe how the underlying cause of the problems was a timing error that was difficult to reproduce. They point out that most computer-related accidents result from requirements faults, not from coding faults, and they list several basic software engineering principles that were violated by the Therac-25:
* Documentation should be done as development progresses, not afterward.
* Software quality assurance practices should be an integral part of the development process...
* Simple designs are easier to understand, code, and test than complex ones.
* Software should be designed to anticipate failures and capture information about them.
* It is not enough to assume that system testing will catch software problems. Software should be tested extensively, as well as subjected to formal analysis, at the component and system levels before integration with the hardware. [10]

A drawback unique to black-box testing is the fact that, since the internals of the component cannot be tested, it is more likely that defects in the code will be propagated unnoticed to other systems. Systems in which reliability or safety is an overriding concern may not be good candidates for black-box reuse for this reason. [12]

question: In what ways do the Object-Oriented characteristics of encapsulation and information hiding support reuse? What kind of criteria would you use in deciding whether to reuse a class in a new system?
answer_guidelines: A good way to answer this question would be to turn it around and begin by thinking of what would make for an ideal reuse situation. Some goals could be: it should be easy for the developer to understand what functionality is available to be reused, related functionalities should be somehow reusable together, it should be easy to understand how to reuse the functionality, and the reusable components should be of high quality. Then, address whether it would be harder or easier to achieve these characteristics in an OO environment, and why.
ground_truth: An ideal reuse situation requires components to be easy to understand, self-contained, and of high quality. Object-oriented characteristics like encapsulation and information hiding make it much easier to achieve these goals. Encapsulation groups related data and the operations that manipulate that data into a single, cohesive unit (an object), ensuring that related functionalities are reusable together [1, 2]. Information hiding ensures that the object's internal implementation details are hidden behind a public interface [3, 4]. This separation means a developer can understand how to reuse the class simply by looking at its well-defined public interface, without needing to decipher its internal code [3, 4]. Furthermore, because these objects hide their design decisions, they are loosely coupled to the rest of the system and can be extracted and reused in new systems with minimal dependency issues [3, 5]. When deciding whether to reuse a class in a new system, key criteria include: does the class perform the function or provide the data you need; if minor modification is required, is it less modification than building the component from scratch; is the component well-documented so you can understand it; and is there a complete record of the component's test and revision history so you can be certain it contains no faults [6].
source_text:
A big advantage of information hiding is that the resulting software units are loosely coupled. The interface to each unit lists the set of access functions that the unit offers, plus the set of other units’ access functions that it uses. This feature makes the software units easier to understand and maintain, because each unit is relatively self-contained. [3]

We call a design object oriented if it decomposes a system into a collection of runtime components called objects that encapsulate data and functionality. [1]

An interface advertises the set of externally accessible attributes and methods. This information is typically limited to public methods, and includes the methods’ signatures, preconditions, postconditions, protocol requirements, and visible quality attributes. Thus, like other interfaces, the interface of an object represents the object’s public face, specifying all aspects of the object’s externally observable behavior. [4]

If you are a consumer for your current project, there are four key characteristics to check about the components you are about to reuse: 1. Does the component perform the function or provide the data you need? 2. If minor modification is required, is it less modification than building the component from scratch? 3. Is the component well-documented, so you can understand it without having to verify its implementation line by line? 4. Is there a complete record of the component’s test and revision history, so you can be certain that it contains no faults? [6]

question: Why is it useful to have separate phases for system and program design?
answer_guidelines: System design gives developers a chance to solidify the broad outlines of the proposed system before having to decide on more specific details of the implementation. Use the beginning of section 6.6 to understand what decisions are reserved for program design. Then consider what negative outcomes could result if developers began debating these issues earlier. What factors, later in development, could make the answers to these issues more or less relevant? Would it ever be a drawback to come to an early decision on these issues, even if the system could be implemented without changing these decisions in later lifecycle phases?
ground_truth: Having separate phases for system design (architecture) and program design (module-level) is highly useful because it allows developers to solidify the broad, system-wide structures, protocols, and interfaces before getting bogged down in specific implementation details [7]. Once the high-level architecture is established, the program design phase can be partitioned into independent work assignments and delegated to separate design teams [8]. As seen in Section 6.6, program design addresses lower-level global issues like specific data management (e.g., mapping classes to object-relational database tables), explicit exception handling constructs, user interface specifics, and the integration of frameworks or toolkits [9-13]. If developers debated these program design issues during early system design, the process would stall due to information overload, and critical high-level architectural flaws might be missed. Later in development, factors like the chosen programming language, available database management systems, or physical hardware constraints make detailed program decisions highly relevant [10, 14]. Making these detailed decisions too early (e.g., locking into a specific GUI toolkit before the system boundary is even defined) is a major drawback because it needlessly constrains the architecture and drastically reduces the design's flexibility if requirements or technologies change later in the lifecycle [8, 13].
source_text:
At this point in the development process, we have an abstract description of a solution to our customer’s problem, in the form of a software architectural design. As such, we have a plan for decomposing the design into software units and allocating the system’s functional requirements to them. The architecture also specifies any protocols that constrain how the units interact with each other, and specifies a precise interface for each unit. [7]

But for the purposes of project management, it is convenient to separate design tasks that require consideration of the entire system from design tasks that pertain to individual software units, because the latter can be partitioned into distinct work assignments and assigned to separate design teams. [8]

There is more to creating the program design than laying out classes and interfaces. We must consider some global issues that may not have been addressed in the architecture. In this section, we explore issues related to data management, exception handling, user interfaces, and frameworks. [9]

The program design must address ways to store and recover persistent objects. Data management takes into account the system requirements concerning performance and space. [10]

Frameworks and patterns are also design aids... However, patterns are not as useful if they constrain a design that needs to be flexible. [15]

question: In section 6.3, a set of questions was given for helping to find potential problems with a set of use cases. Why is it important if two different terms are being used to refer to the same entity?
answer_guidelines: The point of UML diagrams and use cases is to serve as a basis for the eventual detailed design and implementation of the system. Using multiple terms for the same entity hinders using the use cases in this way because it can cause confusion for people reading the diagrams, perhaps leading them to believe that multiple, different entities are being referred to. In your answer, try to think of an example of this situation, and describe the likely results during system development in later lifecycle phases.
ground_truth: It is crucial to avoid using two different terms to refer to the same entity because UML diagrams and use cases form the foundation for the system's detailed design, implementation, and testing. If multiple terms are used, it hinders this translation process by causing confusion among developers and readers, who may mistakenly believe that multiple, distinct entities are required [16, 17]. For example, in the requirements for the Royal Service Station system, the text mentions both a "customer" and a "regular repeat customer" [16]. If designers fail to recognize these as referring to the same fundamental entity concept, they might create two separate, redundant classes in the UML class diagram. During later lifecycle phases, this redundancy would lead to duplicated programming effort, integration problems, inconsistencies in the database (e.g., maintaining two separate tables for customer data), and increased maintenance overhead [16, 17]. It could also cause testing failures if updates to a "customer" do not properly reflect in the "regular repeat customer" object, resulting in missing or unnecessary associations [18]. Consolidating terms ensures the design remains modular and accurately reflects the real-world problem domain without unnecessary duplication.
source_text:
The fifth requirement states that, The system applies only to regular repeat customers. A regular repeat customer means a customer identified by name, address, and birthdate who uses the station’s services at least once per month for at least six months. ... Thus, we have new candidate classes, such as regular repeat customer, name, address, and periodic messages. We can revise Table 6.1 as Table 6.2 to reflect this. However, “regular repeat customer” is redundant, so we can leave it out. [16, 17]

Next, make sure that you check the objects and classes themselves for excesses and deficiencies: missing objects, unnecessary classes, missing or unnecessary associations, or incorrect placement of associations or attributes. Rumbaugh et al. (1991) provide some guidelines to help you identify these conditions during your testing. [18]

question: You are designing a system to help run a bookstore. Revenue for the store comes from two distinct services: customers can purchase books, or bring their books in for rebinding. You are considering making a separate class for each service, both of which would be subclasses of a general “sale item” class. What are the likely benefits of such an approach? Are there any possible arguments against using inheritance in this case? Be sure to specify what factors could influence your decision.
answer_guidelines: The likely benefits involve shared functionality, perhaps for pricing and revenue, which could be implemented once in the superclass and shared in each of these two classes. On the other hand, there are enough differences between the two services that it could be argued that joining such dissimilar classes in an inheritance hierarchy would be confusing, since very little functionality could be shared through the superclass. (For example, book sales might involve updating inventory, tracking the space required for sale, and ordering more copies of a book when it is close to being sold out. Bookbinding would not track inventory but would need another set of methods to deal with expected due dates for the service and scheduling the time of a suitable expert at the shop.) Many factors should be taken into account to make the decision, but in this case a particularly important one is the anticipated future needs of the store. If it is possible that the range of sale items might increase in the future, then it becomes more cost-effective to encapsulate the common functionality in a superclass for sharing among the classes that might be added.
ground_truth: The primary benefit of using an inheritance approach is the ability to encapsulate shared functionality (such as computing price, applying taxes, and tracking revenue) in the "sale item" superclass, allowing both the book purchasing and book rebinding classes to inherit and reuse these common attributes and operations. However, an argument against using inheritance in this case is the low degree of cohesion between the two services. Because book sales involve inventory management and ordering, while bookbinding involves scheduling labor and tracking due dates, the two classes share very little functional similarity. Joining them in an inheritance hierarchy might result in a confusing design where subclasses inherit meaningless or unrelated operations. A key factor influencing the decision is the anticipated future needs of the store. If the bookstore plans to expand its range of sale items or services in the future, using a superclass makes the design more general and cost-effective, providing a reusable template for sharing common functionality among the newly added classes.
source_text:
This kind of construction, called inheritance, defines a new class by directly reusing (and adding to) the definitions of an existing class... A subclass inherits all of the parent class’s attributes, operations, and associations. [1, 2]

Of course, the greatest advantage of inheritance is the ability to change and specialize the behaviors of inherited methods, by selectively overriding inherited definitions. This characteristic helps us to quickly create new types of objects that exhibit new behaviors. [3]

A module that serves multiple purposes is at greater risk of its elements needing to evolve in different ways or at different rates... Instead, our design goal is to make each module as cohesive as possible, so that each module is easier to understand and is less likely to change. [4]

We make a unit more general by increasing the number of contexts in which can it be used. [5]

question: Section 6.7 discusses Chidamber and Kemerer’s metric of depth of inheritance. Why does it seem likely that a class that is deeper in the hierarchy is harder to understand and maintain than one that is less deep?
answer_guidelines: To answer this question, think about how someone reading an OO program knows where the specific definition of a method is located. If there is no inheritance involved, a method is defined within the class to which it belongs. But if that class is part of an inheritance hierarchy, the method need not be defined in the class. Is there any indication of which parent class contains the definition of a method? Can a method be redefined multiple times within a particular hierarchy? How do those factors contribute to the e
ground_truth: A class that is deeper in the inheritance hierarchy is harder to understand and maintain because it inherits attributes and methods from a larger collection of ancestor classes. When a developer reads an object-oriented program, it can be extremely difficult to trace dependencies and pinpoint exactly where a specific method is defined. Because a method can be redefined or overridden multiple times within a deep inheritance hierarchy, and because dynamic binding makes it impossible to statically determine which method will be executed at runtime, the maintainer must manually search through multiple parent and ancestor classes and consider all possibilities. This delocalization of program function across many noncontiguous classes significantly increases the cognitive burden required to comprehend, test, and repair the class.
source_text:
They also consider a class’s depth in the inheritance hierarchy (with depth=1 being the root): the deeper a class is in the hierarchy, the more methods it is likely to inherit. This characteristic makes the class harder to understand and harder to maintain. [6]

...the larger the depth of the class’s inheritance (i.e., attributes and methods are inherited from a larger collection of ancestor classes, thus the class is harder to understand) [7]

Object-oriented techniques may make programs more difficult to understand because of the profusion of program parts. It is hard to discern the original designer’s intent because of delocalization: program plans dispersed throughout many noncontiguous program segments. ... Inheritance can make dependencies difficult to trace. Dynamic binding makes it impossible to determine which of several methods will be executed, so maintainers must consider all possibilities. [8]

question: A stack is a data structure used to store elements. A stack is a last - in, first - out data structure. That is, the last element placed on the stack is the first element that can be removed from the stack. Elements can be placed on or removed from the to p of the stack only. The allowable operations for a stack are empty, full, push, pop and top. The empty operation returns true if there are no elements in the stack, false otherwise. The full operation returns true if the stack is filled to capaci ty, false otherwise. The push operation takes an element as an argument and places the element on top of the stack, if the stack is not full. If the stack is full, the push operation returns an error. The pop operation removes an element from the top of the stack, if the stack is not empty. If the stack is empty, the pop operation returns an error, otherwise, the top element is returned. The top operation returns the element on the top of the stack without removing the element from the stack, if the stack is not empty. The top operation returns an error if the stack is empty. Use an array to implement a stack data structure whose elements are integers. The stack may contain a maximum of 100 elements. Keep in mind the guidelines for programming style that were presented in this chapter.
answer_guidelines: To implement the st ack data structure, you should have followed the guidelines presented in the chapter. You should use meaningful variable names, provide good documentation of your code, use efficient algorithms, and maintain good design principles (low coupling and high c ohesion).
source_text:
The internal documentation contains information directed at someone who will be reading the source code of your programs. Thus, summary information is provided to identify the program and describe its data structures, algorithms, and control flow. Usually, this information is placed at the beginning of each component in a set of comments called the header comment block. [1] ... 
Meaningful Variable Names and Statement Labels. Choose names for your variables and statements that reflect their use or meaning. [3] ... 
Formatting to Enhance Understanding. The format of your comments can help a reader understand the goal of the code and how the goal is reached. Indentation and spacing of statements can reflect the basic control structure. [4]

question: One difficulty with reuse is selecting an appropriate component. Describe a strategy for finding a reusable component. What guidelines or styles would help in the process of selecting a component for reuse?
answer_guidelines: When reusing a component, you may want to examine the documentation, look at the test history, or test the software before you actually commit to using it. It is important to understand whether or not you will have access to the source code, to know who is responsible for changes and to understand the limitations of the reusable component. In Section 7.3, some of the key characteristics you should consider when selecting a reusable component are described. Use this list of characteristics to develop your strategy for selecting a component. How would the strategy be different for white - box versus black - box reuse?
ground_truth:
A strategy for finding and selecting a reusable component involves evaluating four key characteristics: (1) verifying if the component actually performs the needed function or provides the required data; (2) determining if the effort to make minor modifications to the component is less than the effort to build it from scratch; (3) ensuring the component is well-documented so it can be understood without verifying the implementation line by line; and (4) checking for a complete record of the component's test and revision history to guarantee it is fault-free [7].

This strategy changes depending on whether we are engaging in black-box reuse or clear-box (white-box) reuse. In **black-box reuse**, the component is used exactly as is, without modification [8]. Thus, the strategy heavily relies on examining the component's interface, test history, and documentation to be absolutely certain it is failure-free and performs adequately, because the developer will not see or alter the internal code [8, 9]. In **clear-box (white-box) reuse**, the component will be modified to fit specific needs [9]. The strategy here must focus on the component's internal design, modularity, and readability. Because the developer must adapt the code, they must evaluate whether the effort required to understand and safely modify the component's internals is actually less than writing a new equivalent component from scratch [10].
source_text:
If you are a consumer for your current project, there are four key characteristics to check about the components you are about to reuse:
1. Does the component perform the function or provide the data you need?
2. If minor modification is required, is it less modification than building the component from scratch?
3. Is the component well-documented, so you can understand it without having to verify its implementation line by line?
4. Is there a complete record of the component’s test and revision history, so you can be certain that it contains no faults? [7]

As consumers, we can either use a product whole, without modification (called black-box reuse), or modify it to fit our particular needs (called clear-box reuse). We perform black-box reuse often... An important issue in black-box reuse is our ability to verify that a reused module performs its required function in an acceptable way. If the component is code, we want to be sure it is failure-free... [8, 9]

Clear-box reuse (sometimes called white-box reuse) is more common but still controversial, because the effort to understand and modify the component must be less than the effort to write a new equivalent component. [10]

question: Explain the relationship between the design and implementation. Why is it important to match the implementation to the design? What would you do to keep the two consistent?
answer_guidelines: The code should implement the design. Design characteristics such as high cohesion, low coupling and well - defined interfaces should be program characteristics as well. It is important that the design and code match for other activities such as maintenance and testing. To maintain traceability, you may want to include design information in the program comments. Configuration managemen t may also help to maintain consistency between the code and design. Section 7.1 describes the relationship between design and implementation.
ground_truth:
The relationship between design and implementation is one of direct correspondence; the program's code must accurately reflect the structure, algorithms, and interfaces defined in the design documents [11]. It is important to match the implementation to the design because the benefits of good design—such as high cohesion, low coupling, and well-defined interfaces—must carry over into the code to be effective [11]. As the system evolves over its lifetime, developers rely on the design to guide modifications [12]. If the code drifts from the design, subsequent activities like testing, maintenance, and configuration management become virtually impossible because the design documents will no longer accurately map to the system's actual behavior [12].

To keep the two consistent, developers should strictly follow organizational standards and procedures [13]. One practical method is placing comprehensive internal documentation, such as header comment blocks, at the beginning of each code component to explicitly state where the component fits in the general system design [1, 2]. Furthermore, any time the code needs to be restructured differently than the original design, these changes must be reported to the designers and tracked via configuration management so the links among requirements, design, and code can be permanently updated and maintained [14].
source_text:
By structuring code according to standards, you maintain the correspondence between design components and code components. Consequently, changes in design are easy to implement in the code. [13] ... 
The most critical standard is the need for a direct correspondence between the program design components and the program code components. The entire design process is of little value if the design’s modularity is not carried forward into the code. Design characteristics, such as low coupling, high cohesion, and well-defined interfaces, should also be program characteristics, so that the algorithms, functions, interfaces, and data structures can be traced easily from design to code and back again. [11] ... 
Thus, the correspondence between design and code is essential. In later chapters, we will see that testing, maintenance, and configuration management are impossible without the links established by these standards. [12] ...
Just as a good newspaper reporter includes the who, what, where, when, how, and why of a story, you must include the following information in your header comment block for each component: ... 3. where the component fits in the general system design [1, 2] ...
Such changes must be reported to and approved by the designers, so that the links among requirements, design, and code can be documented and maintained. [14]

question: Consider a case where you have attempted to reuse code written by someone else. Wha t kind of reuse was it? What problems did you encounter? How did you resolve the problems? Are there any guidelines in this chapter that may have helped to eliminate or mitigate the problems you encountered?
answer_guidelines: There are many types of problems that may be encountered when you are a consumer of a reusable component. The d ocumentation may be misleading or incorrect. There may be missing functionality that is required by your system. You must determine how to fit a reusable component into the design of the new system. These are only a few of the problems that may occur. Section 7.3 describes characteristics that should be considered when reusing components. Based on the problems that you encountered and the guidelines and programming styles described in this chapter, can you describe ways that the reusable component coul d have been changed to eliminate or mitigate the problems that you encountered?
ground_truth: In a past project, I attempted clear-box reuse (modifying a product to fit particular needs) of a data sorting component [1]. I encountered several problems: the component's internal documentation was missing, the variable names were cryptic (e.g., 'x1' and 'z'), and the input/output dependencies were mixed with the sorting logic. To resolve this, I had to manually trace the execution path to understand the logic before I could safely adapt the code. According to the guidelines in this chapter, these problems could have been mitigated if the producer had followed several rules: using meaningful variable names that reflect their use [2], keeping the component interface general and well-defined, documenting the data structures and algorithms, and separating dependencies so that sections likely to need change are isolated [3]. As a consumer, I should have used the chapter's guidelines to evaluate the component beforehand, checking if it was well-documented enough to understand without having to verify its implementation line by line [4].
source_text: Clear-box reuse (sometimes called white-box reuse) is more common but still controversial, because the effort to understand and modify the component must be less than the effort to write a new equivalent component. [1] ... If you are a consumer for your current project, there are four key characteristics to check about the components you are about to reuse: ... 3. Is the component well-documented, so you can understand it without having to verify its implementation line by line? [4] ... On the other hand, if you are a producer of reusable components, you should keep in mind several things: ... Separate dependencies so that sections likely to need change are isolated from those that are likely to remain the same. Keep the component interface general and well-defined. ... Use clear naming conventions. Document the data structures and algorithms. [3]

question: When writing code, many people are usually i nvolved. Writing code usually requires a great deal of cooperation and coordination. It is important for others to be able to understand what you have written, why you have written it, and how your work fits in with their work. For these reasons, many o rganizations have coding standards and procedures. Using the guidelines from this chapter, write a set of coding standards for a language of your choice. Explain why you have included the standards you have chosen.
answer_guidelines: To write the standards, make the guidelines presented in the chapter operational. For example, to make the guideline of meaningful variable names operational, you might have a standard which requires all variable names to be greater than 5 characters and less than 10 characters. Your reasons for including this standard might be that variable names less than 5 characters are cryptic and anything over 10 characters may be diffic ult to remember.
ground_truth:
For a language like Java, the following coding standards map to the chapter's guidelines:

1. **Header Comment Blocks**: Every class and method must begin with a comment block detailing its name, author, system context, date/revision history, purpose, and data/control structures [5]. Reason: This summary information allows maintenance programmers to locate and understand components easily without reading every line of code [6].
2. **Meaningful Naming Conventions**: Variable names must reflect their specific use (e.g., 'case_counter' instead of 'i3') [7]. Reason: Descriptive names reduce the need for excessive inline comments and lower the risk of introducing faults during modifications [8].
3. **Indentation and Formatting**: Code must use standard indentation (e.g., 4 spaces per block) that reflects the basic control structure [8]. Reason: Formatting visually displays the logic flow, making the code's goal and execution paths significantly easier to read and understand [8].
4. **Localized I/O**: Input and output operations must be localized in separate, specialized components rather than mixed with application logic [9]. Reason: I/O is highly hardware/software dependent, and localization makes these components easier to test, maintain, and adapt to system changes [9].
source_text: Just as a good newspaper reporter includes the who, what, where, when, how, and why of a story, you must include the following information in your header comment block for each component: 1. what your component is called 2. who wrote the component 3. where the component fits in the general system design 4. when the component was written and revised 5. why the component exists 6. how the component uses its data structures, algorithms, and control [5, 10]. ... A maintenance programmer reading blocks like this one will find the component that needs to be changed more easily. [6] ... Choose names for your variables and statements that reflect their use or meaning. [2] ... In fact, the weekwage example is not likely to need comments at all, and you are less likely to introduce faults. [8] ... The format of your comments can help a reader understand the goal of the code and how the goal is reached. Indentation and spacing of statements can reflect the basic control structure. [8] ... Those parts of a program that read input or generate output are highly specialized and must reflect characteristics of the underlying hardware and software. Because of this dependence, the program sections performing input and output functions are sometimes difficult to test. ... Therefore, it is desirable to localize these sections in components separate from the rest of the code. [9]

question: Examine faults from code that you have written . For each fault, identify the type of fault (as in Section 8.1, Types of Faults) and classify the fault using a defect classification. Provide the details of the defect classification used. (You may use the IBM defect classification presented in Table 8.1 or the one from HP illustrated in Figure 8.1.) Describe any difficulties encountered in classifying the faults.
answer_guidelines: Chapter 8 describes many different types of faults. The purpose of this exercise is to give you a better understanding of how these descriptions can be used to identify and understand faults in actual code. It wil l also help you to understand the difficulties in classifying defects. Sometimes it is difficult to classify faults, especially when the defect classification is not orthogonal. The chapter presents two defect classifications. You may use one of th ese classifications or any other, reasonable classification. Be sure your answer clearly describes the defect classification you are using. When answering this question first decide which type of fault you have found. Then, determine if the fault is one of omission or commission. Finally, based on the fault type and your classification of omission or commission, use your defect classification to classify the fault. Did you have difficulties in determining the fault type? Did any of your faults seem to fit in multiple categories?
ground_truth:
(Hypothetical Example based on typical student experience): 
In a recent project, my code crashed with an "Array Index Out of Bounds" exception because I used a <= operator instead of < in a loop boundary condition. 
1. Type of Fault (Section 8.1): This is an 'algorithmic fault' because the algorithm's logic branched/tested the wrong condition.
2. Omission/Commission: It is a fault of 'commission' because the code was present but incorrect (using <= instead of <), rather than missing entirely.
3. Defect Classification (IBM Table 8.1): This falls under 'Algorithm' (fault involving efficiency or correctness of algorithm or data structure) or potentially 'Checking' (fault in program logic that fails to validate data and values properly).
4. Difficulties Encountered: The IBM classification aims to be orthogonal, but it is sometimes ambiguous. Deciding whether an off-by-one loop error is a "Checking" error (failed to validate the array bound) or an "Algorithm" error (the loop logic itself was wrong) was difficult, showing that sometimes faults seem to fit into multiple categories.
source_text:
An algorithmic fault occurs when a component’s algorithm or logic does not produce the proper output for a given input because something is wrong with the processing steps. ... Typical algorithmic faults include branching too soon, branching too late, testing for the wrong condition...

Chillarege et al. (1992) at IBM have developed an approach to fault tracking called orthogonal defect classification... A fault of omission is one that results when some key aspect of the code is missing; for example, a fault may occur when a variable is not initialized. A fault of commission is one that is incorrect; for example, the variable is initialized to the wrong value.

One of the key features of orthogonal defect classification is its orthogonality. That is, a classification scheme is orthogonal if any item being classified belongs to exactly one category... We lose the meaning of the measurements if a fault might belong to more than one class.

TABLE 8.1 IBM Orthogonal Defect Classification
Checking: Fault in program logic that fails to validate data and values properly before they are used
Algorithm: Fault involving efficiency or correctness of algorithm or data structure but not design

question: Based on the faults identified in the previous question, which type of fault occurred most frequently? How might you change your software development approach to eliminate or reduce the occurrence of this type of fault?
answer_guidelines: Answers to this question will vary depending on your fault profile. Your fault profile can be used to identify areas of improvement for yourself. Based on your profile, which type of fault had the highest frequency? Would an y of the techniques described in this chapter or previous chapters be useful in helping you to reduce the frequency of this type of fault? Which type of fault occurs least frequently? Have you done anything in the past to prevent this type of fault from o ccurring?
ground_truth:
(Hypothetical Example extending from the previous question):
Based on my personal fault profile, "Algorithmic faults" (specifically off-by-one errors and incorrect loop boundaries) occur most frequently. To eliminate or reduce the occurrence of these faults, I could change my software development approach by instituting formal 'code inspections' or 'walkthroughs' before I even run the code. Studies show that code inspections can remove up to 85% of faults and are highly effective at finding algorithmic logic errors. Additionally, I could adopt 'open-box testing' strategies, specifically 'statement testing' and 'branch testing', to ensure that I systematically write test cases that execute the loop boundaries (e.g., executing the loop zero times, one time, and the maximum number of times).
source_text:
Algorithmic faults... These faults are sometimes easy to spot just by reading through the program (called desk checking) or by submitting input data from each of the different classes of data that we expect the program to receive...

A code inspection, originally introduced by Fagan (1976) at IBM, is similar to a walkthrough but is more formal. In an inspection, the review team checks the code and documentation against a prepared list of concerns... Fagan (1976) performed an experiment in which 67% of the system’s faults eventually detected were found before unit testing using inspections... Jones (1977) has studied programmer productivity... he found that code inspections removed as many as 85% of the total faults found.

We can also examine the implementation of algorithms... To test code thoroughly, we can choose test cases using at least one of several approaches based on the data manipulated by the code: Statement testing... Branch testing... Path testing...

question: Describe the differences between unit and integration testing. Give the goals for each type of testing and describe when and how each should occur.
answer_guidelines: The main purpose of unit testing is to make sure that the component is functioning properly. The component is tested in isolation to make sure that the inputs produce the expected outputs. The main purpose of integration testing is to verify t hat the system components work together as specified by the design. Integration testing occurs after unit testing. Sections 8.3 and 8.4 describe unit and integration testing in greater detail.
ground_truth:
Unit testing (or module testing) focuses on verifying that an individual program component functions properly in isolation. The goal is to ensure that the component's internal data structures, logic, boundaries, and algorithms correctly convert expected inputs to desired outputs. It should occur first, immediately after the component is coded, and is usually conducted in a controlled environment by feeding predetermined data sets (using open-box and closed-box test strategies) to observe the component's behavior.

Integration testing, on the other hand, is the process of verifying that multiple system components work together correctly as defined by the system and program design specifications. The goal is to ensure that interfaces and information passing between components are handled properly. Integration testing occurs after unit testing. It is conducted by merging unit-tested components according to a specific build strategy—such as bottom-up (testing lowest-level utilities first using drivers), top-down (testing the controlling component first using stubs), or sandwich integration—so that interface faults can be isolated and identified as the system is progressively assembled.
source_text:
First, each program component is tested on its own, isolated from the other components in the system. Such testing, known as module testing, component testing, or unit testing, verifies that the component functions properly with the types of input expected from studying the component’s design. Unit testing is done in a controlled environment whenever possible, so that the test team can feed a predetermined set of data to the component being tested and observe what output actions and data are produced. In addition, the test team checks the internal data structures, logic, and boundary conditions for the input and output data.

When collections of components have been unit-tested, the next step is ensuring that the interfaces among the components are defined and handled properly. Integration testing is the process of verifying that the system components work together as described in the system and program design specifications.

One popular approach for merging components to test the larger system is called bottom-up testing. When this method is used, each component at the lowest level of the system hierarchy is tested individually first. Then, the next components to be tested are those that call the previously tested ones... Many developers prefer to use a top-down approach, which in many ways is the reverse of bottom-up. The top level, usually one controlling component, is tested by itself. Then, all components called by the tested component(s) are combined and tested as a larger unit.

question: Describe the differences b etween object - oriented and traditional testing.
answer_guidelines: Section 8.5 addresses the difference between testing object - or iented systems and traditional systems. Most of the techniques used for traditional testing also apply to object - oriented systems. Object -oriented programs have special characteristics that need several additional steps. Some of the characteristics that must be considered with OO programs that may not be included with traditional testing techniques are: missing objects, unnecessary classes, missing or unnecessary associations, or incorrect placement of associations or attributes. Test case adequacy must also be considered more carefully with OO systems. As Perry and Kaiser (1990) found, when a subclass is added or modified, the inherited methods from the ancestor superclasses must be retested. As noted by Graham (1996a), objects tend to be small and lo w in complexity. However, the complexity often is pushed to the interfaces among components. This shift of complexity means that unit testing may be easier with OO systems, but integration testing must be more extensive.
ground_truth:
While many traditional testing techniques apply to object-oriented (OO) systems, the unique characteristics of OO programs require additional steps. In OO testing, developers must check for excesses and deficiencies specific to object modeling, such as missing objects, unnecessary classes, missing or unnecessary associations, and incorrect placement of associations or attributes. 

Another major difference lies in test case adequacy and inheritance. Encapsulation might make it seem like unmodified reused components need no retesting, but a program adequately tested in isolation may not be adequately tested in combination. Specifically, when a new subclass is added or an existing one is modified, the methods inherited from each of its ancestor superclasses must be retested. 

Finally, Graham (1996a) notes that because objects tend to be small, the complexity that usually resides inside a component in traditional systems is pushed toward the interfaces among components in OO systems. This shift means that unit testing is often easier for OO systems, but integration testing must be much more extensive. Additionally, traditional code coverage measurements are less valuable in OO testing because the interaction of objects, rather than the internal control flow, is the primary source of complexity.
source_text: Next, make sure that you check the objects and classes themselves for excesses and deficiencies: missing objects, unnecessary classes, missing or unnecessary associations, or incorrect placement of associations or attributes. ... Perry and Kaiser (1990) take a careful look at testing object-oriented components... "a program that has been adequately tested in isolation may not be adequately tested in combination" (Perry and Kaiser 1990). In fact, they show that when we add a new subclass or modify an existing subclass, we must retest the methods inherited from each of its ancestor superclasses. ... Graham (1996a) summarizes the differences between object-oriented and traditional testing in two ways. ... objects tend to be small, and the complexity that might ordinarily reside in the component is often pushed instead toward the interfaces among components. This difference means that unit testing is less difficult, but integration testing must be much more extensive. ... Because it is the interaction of objects that is the source of complexity, code coverage measurements and tools are of less value in object-oriented testing than in traditional testing.

question: Choose a piece of code and write test cases for the code to satisfy the requirements of statement testing. Write the test cases for all - uses testing. Which testing strategy is stronger? Which strategy requi res more test cases?
answer_guidelines: Section 8.3 describes the differen t types of test strategies for test thoroughness. Statement testing and all - uses testing are two of the options described. With statement testing, every statement is executed at least once in a test case. With all - uses testing, the test set includes at least one path from every definition to every use that can be reached by the definition. In general, all - uses is stronger and requires more test cases than statement testing.
ground_truth:
Consider the following simple pseudo-code block:
1. read(X)
2. if (X > 0)
3.    Y = X + 1
4. else
5.    Y = 0
6. print(Y)

Statement testing requires that every statement in the component is executed at least once in some test. We can satisfy this with two test cases:
- Test 1: X = 1 (executes statements 1, 2, 3, 6)
- Test 2: X = -1 (executes statements 1, 2, 4, 5, 6)

All-uses testing requires that the test set includes at least one path from every definition of a variable to every use that can be reached by that definition. In this code, X is defined in line 1 and used in lines 2 and 3. Y is defined in lines 3 and 5, and used in line 6.
- Test 1: X = 1 (traces the definition of X to its use in 2 and 3, and the definition of Y in 3 to its use in 6)
- Test 2: X = -1 (traces the definition of X to its use in 2, and the definition of Y in 5 to its use in 6)

While in this exceedingly simple example the two strategies require the same number of test cases, all-uses testing is generally a stronger strategy than statement testing. According to the relative strengths of test strategies (such as Beizer's hierarchy), all-uses testing covers deeper data-flow relationships rather than just executing a line of code. Because it is a stronger strategy, all-uses testing generally requires more test cases than statement testing.
source_text:
To test code thoroughly, we can choose test cases using at least one of several approaches based on the data manipulated by the code:
* Statement testing: Every statement in the component is executed at least once in some test.
* Branch testing: For every decision point in the code, each branch is chosen at least once in some test.
* Path testing: Every distinct path through the code is executed at least once in some test.
* Definition-use path testing: Every path from every definition of every variable to every use of that definition is exercised in some test.
* All-uses testing: The test set includes at least one path from every definition to every use that can be reached by that definition.
... Beizer (1990) describes the relative strengths of these test strategies, as shown in Figure 8.6. For example, testing all paths is stronger than testing all paths from definition to use. In general, the stronger the strategy, the more test cases are involved; we must always consider the trade-off between the resources available for testing and the thoroughness of the strategy we choose.

question: How does system testing differ from unit and integration testing?
answer_guidelines: The emphasis for unit and integration testing is to make sure the code implements the design properly. With system testing, the focus is shifted to the customer. System testing looks to verify that the system implements the requirements properly. For more details on the differences, re - read Section 9.1.
ground_truth: **Unit and integration testing focus on ensuring that the code implements the design properly**; their goal is to verify that the programmers wrote code that successfully does what the designers intended [1]. In contrast, **system testing shifts the focus to the customer, with the objective of ensuring that the system actually does what the customer wants it to do** [1]. Furthermore, during system testing, the system is viewed and tested as a complete, integrated whole rather than as separate pieces or individual components [2].
source_text: The objective of unit and integration testing was to ensure that the code implemented the design properly; that is, that the programmers wrote code to do what the designers intended. In system testing, we have a very different objective: to ensure that the system does what the customer wants it to do. [1] ...In these later steps, the system is viewed and tested as a whole rather than as separate pieces. [2]

question: Explain the purposes and roles of function testing, performance testing, acceptance testing, and installation testing.
answer_guidelines: The purpose and roles of the different types of testing are presented throughout the chapter. For each type of system testing mentioned, describe the purpose and role. Explain when in the system testing process each type of test should occur. Describe how and why each type of test is performed.
ground_truth:
The system testing process occurs in several sequential steps, each with a distinct purpose:
1. **Function testing:** This occurs first. Its purpose is to **check that the integrated system performs its functions exactly as specified in the developers' requirements specification** [3].
2. **Performance testing:** Occurring after function tests are successful, performance testing **compares the integrated components against the nonfunctional system requirements** (e.g., security, accuracy, speed, and reliability) to ensure these constraints are met [4]. 
3. **Acceptance testing:** After the developers finish performance testing, the customer evaluates the system. Its purpose is to **ensure the system meets the customer's understanding of the requirements**, assuring them that the requested system was actually built [5]. It is performed by the customers and users, sometimes as a benchmark or pilot test [6-8].
4. **Installation testing:** This is the final round of testing, performed at the user's actual site [9]. Because acceptance tests are often run in a different test facility, installation testing allows users to exercise the system in its actual environment. It focuses on **verifying the completeness of the installed system and checking any characteristics that might be affected by specific site conditions** [5, 9].
source_text:
Initially, we test the functions performed by the system. We begin with a set of components that were tested individually and then together. A function test checks that the integrated system performs its functions as specified in the requirements. [3]
Once the test team is convinced that the functions work as specified, the performance test compares the integrated components with the nonfunctional system requirements. [4]
The customers also test the system, making sure that it meets their understanding of the requirements... This test, called an acceptance test, assures the customers that the system they requested is the system that was built for them. [5]
The final round of testing involves installing the system at user sites. If acceptance testing has been performed on-site, installation testing may not be needed. However, if the acceptance testing conditions were not the same as the actual site conditions, additional testing is necessary... The tests focus on two things: completeness of the installed system and verification of any functional or nonfunctional characteristics that may be affected by site conditions. [9]

question: What is the difference between verification and validation? Which types of testing address verification? Which types of testing address validation?
answer_guidelines: A verified system implies that the system operates the way the designers intended it to operate. A validated system implies that the system meets the customers ' expectations. The various types of unit and integration tests focus on verification. System testing focuses on validation. Review the descriptions of the types of unit and integration tests and the types of system testing to determine which tests contribute to verification and validation. Section 9.1 contains more information on verification and validation.
ground_truth:
The difference between verification and validation is best summarized by the phrase: **verification ensures that we build the system right, whereas validation ensures that we build the right system** [10]. A **verified system** operates exactly the way the designers intended it to operate, representing the designers' correct interpretation of the requirements specification [5]. A **validated system**, on the other hand, is one that meets the customer's actual expectations and solves their fundamental problem [5]. 

**Unit testing, integration testing, function testing, and performance testing address verification**, as they ensure the code correctly implements the design and the developer's formal specifications [5, 11]. **Acceptance testing addresses validation**, as it is conducted by the customer to compare the verified system against their original requirements definition to ensure their needs are truly met [5, 11].
source_text:
To summarize, verification ensures that we build the system right, whereas validation ensures that we build the right system! [10]
At this point, the system operates the way the designers intend. We call this a verified system; it is the designers’ interpretation of the requirements specification. Next, we compare the system with the customer’s expectations by reviewing the requirements definition document. If we are satisfied that the system we have built meets the requirements, then we have a validated system... [5]
Unit and integration testing addresses the correctness of programs... system testing should verify the system design... Acceptance testing, which is conducted by the customer rather than the developer, validates the requirements... [11]

question: Describe the principles of Cleanroom and how it differs from conventional testing.
answer_guidelines: Cleanroom reflects the ideas used in the manufacturing of chips. The goal is to keep faults at a minimum. For software, the goals are to certify software before unit testing and to produce as few faults as possible. With Cleanroom, verification replaces unit testing. Cleanroom also makes use of statistical testing. Section 9.9 describes the principles and the advantages and drawbacks to the Cleanroom process in greater detail. Use this information to compare the Cleanroom process against traditional testing.
ground_truth: The Cleanroom process reflects ideas used in chip production to keep faults at a minimum [1]. It is based on two fundamental principles: first, to certify the software with respect to the specifications rather than waiting for unit testing to find faults, and second, to produce zero-fault or near-zero-fault software [2]. Cleanroom differs from conventional testing primarily because formal verification and correctness proofs take the place of unit testing, which is strictly not permitted [3]. After the software is certified against its specification, the process uses statistical usage testing, where test cases are randomized based on the probability of usage [4]. Cleanroom proponents argue that traditional coverage testing finds faults in a random order, whereas statistical testing is much more effective at improving overall software reliability and extending the mean time to failure [4].
source_text: In the mid-1980s, researchers at IBM proposed a new software development process, designed to produce high-quality software with a high-productivity team. Their process, called cleanroom, reflects ideas used in chip production to keep faults at a minimum (Mills, Dyer, and Linger 1987). ... The cleanroom approach addresses two fundamental principles: 1. to certify the software with respect to the specifications, rather than wait for unit testing to find the faults 2. to produce zero-fault or near-zero-fault software ... This verification takes the place of unit testing, which is not permitted. At this stage, the software is certified with respect to its specification. The final step involves statistical usage testing, where test cases are randomized based on probability of usage, as we saw earlier in this chapter. ... The researchers at IBM feel that traditional coverage testing finds faults in random order, whereas statistical testing is more effective at improving overall software reliability. Cobb and Mills (1990) report that statistical usage testing is more than 20 times as effective at extending MTTF than is coverage testing.

question: Read the press release and failure report for the Ariane - 5 Flight 501. An electronic copy of the failure report is available at http://www.esrin.esa.it/htdocs/tidc/Press/Press96/ariane5rep.html . The joint ESA/CNES press release is available at http://www.esrin.esa.it/htdocs/tidc/Pres/Press96/pres19.html . What kinds of tests might have exposed the problems that caused each of the failures?
answer_guidelines: The most obvious failure from the Ariane - 5 flight was the explosion of the space rocket itself. The failure report describes additional faults and failures that contributed to the explosion. Use the descriptions of the types of testing in this chapter to determine which tests may have uncovered the faults. Explain how the testing would have uncovered the fault. Be sure to consider whether or not the type of test you describe would have been feasible.
ground_truth: The Ariane-5 explosion was caused by an overflow error in the reused Inertial Reference System (SRI) software [5, 6]. A major reason the testing process failed to expose this was that no test was performed to verify how the SRI would behave when subjected to the actual flight time sequence and trajectory of Ariane-5 [7]. Proper function testing and requirements validation could have exposed the flaw, but the specification for the SRI software completely omitted the Ariane-5 trajectory data from its functional requirements [7]. Additionally, closed-loop system simulation tests might have exposed the error if the actual SRI equipment had been tested instead of merely relying on software models [5]. The engineers ran closed-loop simulations of the complete flight, but they simulated the SRI in software instead of using the actual equipment because the SRI had a 1-millisecond base period while the simulation facility used 6 milliseconds [5, 8]. The investigative board noted that while incorporating the actual SRI into the simulation would have compromised simulation accuracy, such an integration test was highly feasible and necessary to prove the proper system integration of the equipment under realistic application constraints [9].
source_text: The investigators reported that “no test was performed to verify that the SRI would behave correctly when being subjected to the count-down and flight time sequence and the trajectory of Ariane-5” (Lions et al. 1996). In fact, the specification for the SRI software did not contain the Ariane-5 trajectory data among its functional requirements. ... At this facility, engineers ran closed-loop simulations of the complete flight, including ground operations, telemetry flow, and launcher dynamics. ... During these tests, the actual SRIs were not used; instead, the SRIs were simulated using specially developed software. Only some open-loop tests were performed with the actual SRI, and just for electrical integration and communication compliance. ... So why were the SRIs not used in the closed-loop simulation? ... simulating failures modes was thought to be better with a model. Finally, the SRI operated with a base period of one millisecond, but the Functional Simulation Facility used six milliseconds, further reducing simulation precision. ... While high accuracy of a simulation is desirable, in the FSF system tests it is clearly better to compromise on accuracy but achieve all other objectives, amongst them to prove the proper system integration of equipment such as the SRI.

question: Examine t he documentation and training resources for a software system of your choice.  What resources are available to the users?  What resources are available to the operators?
answer_guidelines: This exercise is intended to help you to i dentify the different training and documentation resources available with software systems. Use the descriptions of training and documentation presented in this chapter to help you identify the resources available with the system of your choice.  You shoul d be able to distinguish between resources that are meant for users and those resources that are meant for operators.
ground_truth: For a hypothetical Content Management System (CMS), the available resources vary based on the roles defined in the system. A user exercises the main system functions (like publishing content), while an operator performs supplementary tasks to support the major work (like database backups) [1, 2]. For users, resources would include User's Manuals that describe the system's purpose and how to perform primary functions such as document creation, modification, and deletion [3, 4]. They would also have access to online help, icons, and tutorials for navigating the interface [5-7]. For operators, resources would include Operator's Manuals that detail hardware and software configurations, procedures for granting or denying access to users, and techniques for duplicating or backing up files [8]. Operator training and documentation focus on how the system works and how to support the users, rather than the primary business tasks [9].
source_text:
A user exercises the main system functions to help solve the problem described by the requirements definition document. Thus, a user is a problem solver for the customer. However, a system often has supplementary tasks that support major system functions. ... an operator performs these functions to support the major work. [2]

Operators are trained on two levels: how to bring up and run the new system, and how to support users. ... operators concentrate on the particulars of the developed system: how to recover lost files or documents, how to communicate with other systems, and how to invoke a variety of support procedures. [9]

A user's manual is a reference guide or tutorial for system users. The manual should be complete and understandable, so sometimes it presents the system to users in layers... [3]

Operator's manuals present material to operators in the same fashion as user's manuals. The intended audience is the only difference between the operator's manual and the user's manual: users want to know the details of system function and use, and operators want to know the details of system performance and access. Thus, the operator's manual explains hardware and software configurations, methods for granting and denying access to a user, procedures for adding or removing peripherals from a system, and techniques for duplicating or backing up files and documents. [8]

question: What kinds of training aids and resources would be useful for users with little or no computer experience?  Give examples of aids or documentation meant for novice users.
answer_guidelines: In this exercise, you should focus on the training and documentation aids appropriate for novice users.  Throughout the chapter, there ar e descriptions of various resources.  When reviewing these descriptions, think about how each resource described might be useful for an inexperienced user.  Try to find examples of support for novice users in software systems that you have used.
ground_truth: Users with little or no computer experience benefit from resources that minimize task interference and do not require memorizing complex commands. Useful training aids include graphical icons, which make functions easily remembered by simply scanning the interface, and online help with hypermedia to provide detailed, searchable assistance [5, 6]. Demonstrations and classes using a 'show-and-tell' approach, where novices can watch a presentation and then try the function on their own computers, are highly effective [10]. Utilizing 'expert users' as role models and floating instructors is also very useful, as it proves to novices that the system can be mastered and provides a comfortable, non-threatening source for answering questions [11, 12]. Finally, layered documentation, such as providing a distinct 'Gentle Introduction' manual, is perfectly suited to ensure the novice computer user is not overwhelmed by technical details [13].
source_text:
Most computer-based systems follow the example of Apple and Xerox in using icons to depict a user's choice of system functions. A click of the mouse selects an icon, and a second click invokes the function. Training for such systems is relatively simple, since the functions are more easily remembered by scanning the icons than by recalling commands and syntax. [5]

Demonstrations and classes can be more flexible and dynamic than paper or online documents. Users may prefer a show-and-tell approach, whereby they try to exercise a demonstrated function. [10]

Sometimes it is not enough to see a demonstration or participate in a class. You need a role model to convince you that you can master the system. In this case, it is useful to designate one or more users and operators as "expert." ... Thus, experts convince the students that the impossible is really possible. [11]

Each book is designed for a different audience and has a different purpose... A Gentle Introduction to S-PLUS for the novice computer user... [13]

question: Suppose a system has functions that are rarely executed by users or operators.  What types of resources would be appropriate for these rarely used functions?
answer_guidelines: The knowle dge gained in training can be forgotten easily over time if the system functions are not exercised regularly.  There are several training options that may be useful in this situation.  You should review the different resources described throughout the chap ter and comment on the resources that would be useful in this situation.  In addition, you may want to include examples from your own experiences.
ground_truth: When functions are rarely executed, the knowledge gained during initial training can be easily forgotten by both users and operators [14]. To support these infrequent tasks, developers should provide formal documentation, like system manuals, which act as reference guides (similar to a car owner's manual) to be used specifically when a question or problem arises [15]. Online help functions are also highly appropriate, allowing the user to search for and read about a function's purpose directly on the screen without needing to locate a paper document [6, 16]. Finally, quick reference guides—which are one- or two-page summaries of primary functions kept at the workstation—are extremely useful for helping users quickly recall how to perform these functions without having to read lengthy explanations [17].
source_text:
The knowledge gained in training can be easily forgotten for those system functions that are not exercised regularly. ... Without review training, users and operators tend to perform only the functions with which they feel comfortable; they may not use other system functions that can make them more efficient and productive. [14]

Formal documentation accompanies every system and supports training. ... The system manuals are often similar to an automobile owner's manual; they are references to be used when a problem or question arises. [15]

Many users prefer to have automated assistance at their fingertips, rather than having to locate a reference guide of some kind to help them. Some systems include an online help function. [16]

A useful intermediate measure is a quick reference guide. This summary of primary system functions and their use is designed to be a one-or two-page reminder that users or operators can keep at the workstation. By referring to the guide, a user can find out how to perform commonly used or essential functions without having to read a lengthy explanation of how each works. [17]

question: Often, the tr aining and documentation needs for novice users are very different from the needs of expert users.  Give examples of cases where training aids or documentation for one user group are inappropriate for the other.
answer_guidelines: To answer this question, review the training aids and documentation examples presented throughout the chapter .  As you review the resources, think of examples that you have experienced in software that you have used.  Categorize these examples as resources for novices or resources for expert users.  Use this information to describe reasons why resources for one g roup may not be appropriate for the other user group.  For example, “wizards” may be useful for novices; however expert users may find them cumbersome to use.  Similarly, documentation meant for expert users may be incomprehensible to novice users.
ground_truth: A novice user requires training aids and documentation that assume little to no prior knowledge, such as introductory tutorials, graphical icons, and step-by-step "wizards". An example of this is a "Gentle Introduction" manual designed specifically for novices. However, if an expert user is forced to use a wizard, they often find it cumbersome and inefficient, because it prevents them from utilizing shortcuts or advanced settings. Conversely, documentation designed for an expert user, such as a "Programmer's Manual" or a technical Crash Course, contains specialized jargon and assumes a deep understanding of computer concepts. Providing this expert-level documentation to a novice would be entirely inappropriate, as it would be incomprehensible and overwhelming, leading to frustration. This is why training must be layered and individualized to accommodate variations in backgrounds and preferences, allowing experienced users to skip basic modules that novices require.
source_text:
We must begin our design of the complete set of documentation by considering the intended audience. Manuals and guides can be written for users, operators, systems support people, or others.

For example, the S-PLUS package... came with several books... Each book is designed for a different audience and has a different purpose... 
* A Gentle Introduction to S-PLUS for the novice computer user
* A Crash Course in S-PLUS for the experienced computer user
* S-PLUS Programmer’s Manual, explaining the S and S-PLUS programming languages

Individualized systems often accommodate this variation in backgrounds, experience, and preference... Since backgrounds vary, different training modules can address different types of students. Users who know how to use a keyboard can skip the modules on typing, and operators who are well-versed in computer concepts need not study the module on what each peripheral does.

question: Think about experiences you have had with training and documentation resources. Are there any cases where these aids have interfered with your usage of the software system? If so, how would you change the documentation or training aid?
answer_guidelines: The answers to this question will vary based upon your experiences with training aids and resources. Use the chapter descriptions to help you identify different types of training aids. Have you encountered resources that have hindered you use of a software system? For example, was the user documentation incorrect? Were there aids that were meant for novice users that you were unable to disable? Was information missing from the documentation that made the system impossible to use? Once you have answered these questions, think about things that you would do to eliminate some of the problems that you have encountered.
ground_truth:
In my experience, I have encountered unskippable, highly restrictive automated tutorials (simulations) in software that forced me to complete basic steps before accessing the actual system. Because I was already familiar with similar systems, this aid caused "task interference" and hindered my usage. As Oppenheimer points out, when users have to concentrate on manipulating the training software instead of the subject at hand, learning can diminish. I have also encountered poorly written, monolithic paper manuals that lacked an index or search function, making it impossible to find specific commands quickly.

To eliminate these problems, I would change the training modules to be strictly optional or layered, allowing users with more experience to bypass basic introductions. To fix the documentation issues, I would convert the monolithic paper manual into a hypermedia-based online help system or add comprehensive navigation aids—such as glossaries, tabs, color-coding, and cross-referencing—to the paper manual so users can find what they need without reading lengthy, irrelevant explanations.
source_text:
Training relates how the functions are performed now (with the existing system) to how they will be performed later with the new one. Doing so is difficult, because users are often forced to block out familiar activities in order to learn new ones. (Psychological studies call this task interference.)

Oppenheimer (1997) points out that as users “concentrate on how to manipulate software instead of on the subject at hand, learning can diminish rather than grow.” He explains that the use of techniques such as simulation can hide many of the assumptions we are making...

A complete and thorough user’s manual is useless if you cannot find needed information quickly and easily. A poorly written user’s manual results in frustrated users who are uncomfortable with the system and may not use it as effectively as possible. Thus, any techniques to enhance readability or access to information are helpful, such as glossaries, tabs, numbering, cross-referencing, color coding, diagrams, and multiple indices.

Sophisticated online help that makes use of hypermedia technology allows the user to delve into collections of related online documents for as much detail as needed for understanding.

question: The “Millenium Bug” or “Y2K problem” is perhaps the most infamous software maintenance problem. Many computer systems represent the year as only two digits and are expected to have problems in the year 2000, when the value for the new year (“00”) is suddenly less rather than greater than the value for the previous year (“99”). Find a discussion of the Y2K problem written for nonscientists, for example, in a newspaper or popular magazine. How many of the maintenance problems listed in section 11.3 are accurately presented in the article? Are there issues in section 11.3 that contribute to the Y2K problem but are not given in the article?
answer_guidelines: Many of the issues in section 11.3 relate in some way to the Y2K problem. Some examples: The limits of human understanding are certainly applicable. There is a definite limit to how quickly maintainers can approach a system that is unfamiliar to them and understand enough about it to make the correct changes for a maintenance problem. That difficulty is compounded when the system being maintained is old and the chances of missing documentation or even source code have increased.
